title: Two Sum
summary: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

examples.explanation: nums[0] + nums[1] = 2 + 7 = 9, so return [0, 1].

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Read the problem aloud. Restate it in your own words. Then start asking clarifying questions BEFORE you write a line of code. This signals seniority and prevents 5 minutes of wrong-direction work.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of the array?
clarifying_questions.why: Determines acceptable complexity. n ≤ 10⁴ → O(n²) brute force is OK. n ≤ 10⁶ → need O(n) or O(n log n). The constraint dictates the algorithm.

clarifying_questions.question: Are negative numbers, zeros, or duplicates allowed?
clarifying_questions.why: Negatives are fine for hash-map (any int can be a key) but break some range-based tricks. Zeros mean a value can be its own complement (target=0, value=0). Duplicates mean the same value can appear at different indices — the answer might pair two equal numbers (e.g., [3,3] target 6).

clarifying_questions.question: Is exactly one valid answer guaranteed, or could there be zero / many?
clarifying_questions.why: If guaranteed unique, you can return on the first match. If multiple are valid, the question changes ("any one" vs "all pairs"). If none guaranteed, you need a sentinel return value — usually an empty list or [-1, -1].

clarifying_questions.question: Can I modify the input array?
clarifying_questions.why: Some algorithms (sort-then-two-pointer) mutate. Interviewers may expect immutability for code-cleanliness reasons. Always ask — costs nothing, prevents wasted work if they say "no".

clarifying_questions.question: What should I return — indices, values, or a boolean?
clarifying_questions.why: Two Sum returns indices, but the variants don't. "Two Sum II" returns 1-indexed positions. "Two Sum IV — Input is a BST" returns a boolean. Misreading the return spec is a classic interview face-plant.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Start with brute force. Estimate its complexity. Then explain how to do better. State your tradeoff (extra space for time). Implement the simpler-to-explain version, not the cleverest. Test on the given example first.

edge_cases.details: Constraint says n ≥ 2. Test [1,2] with target 3 — does it return [0,1]?
Test [3,3] with target 6 — your hash map must NOT block the second 3 from finding the first. Most off-by-one bugs hide here.
Test [-1, 1, 0] with target 0. Does complement = target - x work when target is 0 or negative? It should — but verify.
What if the answer is the first two elements ([1,2,...] target 3) or the last two ([...,9,10] target 19)? Walk through both mentally.
Constraint says "may not use the same element twice." A hash map naturally enforces this if you check the map BEFORE inserting the current value. Confirm your ordering.
Problem says exactly one solution is guaranteed, so this shouldn't happen. But if asked to extend the problem to handle "no answer", what would you return? Knowing the answer signals seniority.

common_mistakes: Forgetting that the same element can't be used twice — using nums[i] + nums[i].
Off-by-one when returning indices — returning the value instead of the index.
Hashing the array as a value lookup but losing track of original indices.
Returning [seen[complement], i] vs [i, seen[complement]] — the order doesn't matter per the spec, but be intentional about it.

faang.introduction: Here's a warm-up. Given an array of integers and a target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to target. Each input has exactly one solution, and you can't use the same element twice.
faang.introduction_context: Often the first question in a 45-min loop, or a warm-up before a harder follow-up (3Sum, 4Sum, Two Sum II).
really_testing: Can you turn a vague prompt into a clear contract by asking the right clarifying questions?
Do you instinctively think in complexity tradeoffs (brute force → optimization)?
Can you write clean code under mild pressure (naming, structure, no off-by-ones)?
Do you test your own code on the given example without being asked?
Can you narrate your thought process — "think out loud"?

rubric.criteria: Asked 3+ clarifying Qs without prompting. Stated brute force first, immediately proposed hash-map optimization. Walked through the example before AND after coding. Clean code with semantic names. Discussed edge cases (duplicates, negatives) unprompted. Total time: ~10 min.
Reached the optimal O(n) solution. May have skipped 1 clarifying question or 1 edge case. Code is correct but might have a minor stylistic issue. Total time: 15-20 min.
Jumped to coding without asking questions. Confused indices vs values mid-solution. Needed a nudge to think about the hash map. Tested only after being asked.
Couldn't reach O(n) even with hints. Couldn't trace through an example. Wrote buggy code and didn't recognize the bugs when reading their own code. Stayed silent for long stretches.

playbook: Restate the problem in your own words. ("I need indices i, j with nums[i] + nums[j] = target, can't reuse the same index. Right?")
Ask 2-3 clarifying questions from the list in Interview Tips above. Don't ask 10 — interviewers find that excessive.
Propose brute force out loud. "Simplest: check every pair. O(n²) time, O(1) space." Don't write it — just state it.
State the optimization. "I think we can do O(n) with a hash map. Tradeoff: O(n) extra space."
Walk through ONE example before coding. Show how the hash map fills up and detects the pair. This proves to the interviewer you actually understand it.
Code cleanly. Variable names: seen, complement. Not d, x, tmp.
Test on the given example AFTER coding. Walk through it. Confirm output matches expected.
Mention edge cases you'd handle if pushed: duplicates (e.g., [3,3]), no-answer (return what?), large n. Interviewer may probe one.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min for a strong run. Finish faster and they'll ask a follow-up (3Sum, sorted array variant, return all pairs, etc.) — be ready.
dont_do: Don't code immediately. A pause to think is mature, not weak.
Don't overoptimize prematurely. Don't try sorting + binary search — go straight to hash map.
Don't return values instead of indices. Read the spec twice.
Don't forget the "same element" rule. nums[i] + nums[i] is NOT valid.
Don't go silent when stuck. Narrate your thinking — interviewers can't help you if they don't know where you are.
Don't argue with the interviewer. If they say "what if there are duplicates?", explore it. They're checking how you handle pushback.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair. Slow but correct — useful as the obvious first approach you propose in an interview before optimizing.

approaches.name: Hash Map
approaches.explanation: For each number, check if its complement (target - x) has already been seen. If yes, we found our pair. If no, store the current number with its index. Single pass, O(n) time at the cost of O(n) space.

title: Contains Duplicate
summary: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return false if every element is distinct.

examples.explanation: 1 appears twice.
All elements are distinct.
Many duplicates.

test_cases.label: duplicate at end of large array

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Easy warm-up — but use it to establish good habits. Restate, ask a clarifying question or two, propose the obvious O(n) hash-set approach, code it, test it. This is interview-style scaffolding for harder questions to come.

clarifying_questions.question: How big can n be?
clarifying_questions.why: n=10^5 means O(n²) brute force is too slow (10^10 ops). Need at least O(n log n) sort, ideally O(n) hash set.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I just detect duplicates or count how many?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec returns bool. Variants ask for the duplicate value, the count, or all duplicates. Always read carefully.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I use extra memory, or is O(1) space a requirement?
clarifying_questions.why: If O(1) space, you're forced to sort first (O(n log n)) and check adjacent. Otherwise, hash set is the clean answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Are negative numbers or zeros possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per constraints. Still works as hash keys, but worth confirming so you handle edge values like 0 and INT_MIN correctly.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the input be modified?
clarifying_questions.why: If yes, you can sort in place. If no, you need extra space or a copy to sort.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: State brute force out loud — O(n²) pairs. Won't pass n=10^5. Propose hash set: walk through array, return True the moment a value is re-encountered. O(n) time, O(n) space. The early-return matters when the duplicate appears near the start of a big array.

edge_cases.details: [1] → false. n=1 means no possible duplicate. Confirm your loop handles this.
[1,2,3,4,5] → false. Function returns false only after fully scanning — don't early-return false incorrectly.
[1,1,1,1] → true on the second 1. Early termination matters.
[1,2,3,...,n,1] → true only when you hit the last element. Worst case for early-termination.
Stress test: n=10^5 distinct values. Pure brute force is 10^10 ops — times out. Confirm your O(n) handles it.

common_mistakes: Using a list instead of a set to check membership → `if x in list` is O(n), making total O(n²).
Returning len(set(nums)) != len(nums) — correct but loses the early-termination benefit on huge inputs with early duplicates.
Forgetting that the function returns bool, not the duplicate value.
Off-by-one or wrong loop bounds in a brute force attempt.

faang.introduction: Easy warm-up. Given an array, return true if any value appears more than once.
faang.introduction_context: Usually a 5-10 minute warm-up before a harder follow-up. Common follow-ups: Contains Duplicate II (within k indices), Longest Consecutive Sequence (uses a hash set in a clever way), or Two Sum (related hash-map pattern).
really_testing: Comfort with hash sets as a default O(n) tool for membership/duplicate detection.
Awareness of time/space tradeoffs (hash set vs sort-and-scan).
Can you reach the right tool without overthinking the easy problem?
Do you write the early-return that matters for large inputs?

rubric.criteria: Immediately reaches hash set with early return. States the time/space tradeoff out loud. Mentions sort + adjacent-scan as the O(1)-space alternative when probed. Codes cleanly in 3-5 min.
Reaches hash set, possibly after stating brute force first. Clean code. May miss the early-return optimization but handles correctness.
Stuck at O(n²) brute force, or confused about set membership being O(1). Or returns wrong answer on edge cases.
Couldn't solve in 15+ minutes. Or made the code unreasonably complex for an easy problem.

playbook: Restate the problem aloud — 'return true if any value appears more than once'.
Ask about n size and space constraints. Listen for the answer; it shapes your approach.
State brute force out loud: 'Nested loop, O(n²). Won't scale.' Don't code it.
Propose hash set: 'Single pass, add to set, return true on collision. O(n) time, O(n) space.'
Code it. Use a `for x in nums` loop, check `if x in seen`, return True, else add.
Test on the early-duplicate case ([1,1,...]) and the no-duplicate case ([1,2,...,n]).
If asked: mention sort + adjacent-scan as the O(1)-space alternative. O(n log n) but no extra memory.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-8 min for a strong run. The interview's real signal is whether you propose the right solution immediately or fumble.
dont_do: Don't actually code brute force — just state it and move on. Wastes interview time.
Don't use `len(set(nums)) != len(nums)` for huge arrays — it loses early termination.
Don't use a list for membership checks — it's O(n) per check.
Don't overthink an easy problem. Hash set is the canonical answer.
Don't forget to return False at the end if the loop completes.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair. Correct but quadratic — won't scale to n=10^5. Only useful as the obvious 'first thought' to state before optimizing.

approaches.name: Hash Set
approaches.explanation: Walk through the array once. For each value, check if it's been seen before (O(1) average with a hash set). If yes, return True immediately — early termination saves time on inputs where the duplicate appears early. If we finish the loop, all values are distinct.

approaches.name: Sort + Adjacent Scan (O(1) space)
approaches.explanation: When extra space isn't allowed, sort the array first — duplicates become adjacent. Then scan for any two consecutive equal values. O(n log n) time from the sort, O(1) extra space (in-place sort). Mention this when interviewer asks 'can you do it without extra space?'

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
summary: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day. You want to maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and choosing a different day in the future to sell that stock. Return the maximum profit you can achieve from this transaction. If you cannot achieve any profit, return 0.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 (price = 1) and sell on day 5 (price = 6), profit = 6-1 = 5.
Prices are decreasing — no profitable transaction. Return 0.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Track min-so-far and max-profit-so-far as you scan once. The key insight: today's max profit is today's price minus the minimum I've seen up to today. Walk through one example before coding — interviewers love seeing you trace the algorithm.

clarifying_questions.question: How many transactions am I allowed?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says ONE. The 'II', 'III', 'IV' variants allow multiple — completely different algorithms. Always confirm.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I buy and sell on the same day?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says 'different day in the future'. So no. The constraint forces single-pass logic.

clarifying_questions.question: What if no profit is possible (prices strictly decreasing)?
clarifying_questions.why: Return 0 — NOT a negative number. Common bug: returning the smallest possible loss.

clarifying_questions.question: Can prices be negative or zero?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, 0 <= prices[i] <= 10^4. No negatives. But knowing the bounds confirms an int doesn't overflow.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of n?
clarifying_questions.why: 10^5. O(n²) brute force times out. Need O(n).

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Brute force: try every (buy_day, sell_day) pair where sell_day > buy_day. O(n²). Better: as I scan, I keep track of the lowest price seen so far. At each day, the max profit I could realize is today's_price minus that min. Track the best across all days. Single pass, O(n) time, O(1) space.

edge_cases.details: [5] → 0. Can't buy AND sell with one day. Confirm your loop handles n=1.
[7,6,4,3,1] → 0. The min keeps decreasing, but the profit (today - min) never becomes positive.
[5,5,5] → 0. Profit on each day is 0, not negative.
[3,2,1,2,3,4] → 3. Buy at 1, sell at 4. Your min should land on 1 before seeing 4.
[5,1,5,1,5] → 4. Min stays at 1, max profit is 5-1=4.

common_mistakes: Returning a negative number when prices decrease — should return 0 per spec.
Updating min_price AFTER computing the profit for today — off-by-one. Compute profit FIRST, then maybe update min.
Trying to apply Kadane's directly — related but distinct (Kadane is for sums of subarrays, this is for max gap forward).
Sorting the prices — destroys the time order; you'd lose 'buy before sell' meaning.
Off-by-one initializing min_price (using prices[0] vs INT_MAX). Both work but mixing them is confusing.

faang.introduction: Stock problem. Given an array of daily prices, find the max profit from one buy-sell pair (sell after buy).
faang.introduction_context: Universal warm-up at FAANG. Tests the O(n²) → O(n) single-pass optimization. Sets up follow-ups in 'II' (multiple transactions), 'III' (at most 2), 'IV' (at most k).
really_testing: Single-pass thinking: 'as I scan, what's the min I've seen and the best profit so far?'
Recognition that this is forward-only (sell after buy — can't sort).
Returning 0 when no profit possible (not negative).
Articulating the recurrence ('today's profit = today's price - min seen so far').

rubric.criteria: Immediately states brute force O(n²) then derives O(n) single-pass tracking. Initializes correctly. Tests on the decreasing array to confirm 0 return. Clean code. ~7 min.
Reaches O(n) after stating brute force. Handles the no-profit case correctly. May miss one edge case until prompted.
Stuck at O(n²). Or returns a negative number on decreasing input. Or tries to sort.
Can't reach O(n) with hints. Sells before buys. Returns wrong answer with no recognition.

playbook: Restate the problem aloud — 'find max profit from ONE buy-sell pair, sell must be after buy'.
Ask: 'How many transactions?' — confirms it's one.
Ask: 'What if no profit is possible?' — confirms return 0.
State brute force out loud: nested loop over all (buy, sell) pairs. O(n²).
Derive O(n): 'As I walk through, I track the minimum price I've seen so far. At each day, the best profit I could realize selling today is today's price minus that min.'
Initialize: min_price = prices[0], best_profit = 0.
Code carefully: walk prices, update best_profit = max(best_profit, p - min_price), then min_price = min(min_price, p).
Test on [7,6,4,3,1] → should return 0 (decreasing).
Test on [7,1,5,3,6,4] → should return 5 (buy at 1, sell at 6).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 7-10 min. Follow-up almost always is II (multiple transactions) or III (at most 2). Be ready to discuss DP for those.
dont_do: Don't use Kadane's directly — wrong tool. Kadane's max-subarray-sum is for contiguous subarrays, not for max gap.
Don't return negative on decreasing input. Spec says return 0.
Don't sort the array — destroys time order. You CANNOT sell before you buy.
Don't track 'max so far' and subtract — leads to selling-before-buying bugs.
Don't initialize best_profit = negative infinity. Initialize to 0.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every (buy_day, sell_day) pair where sell_day > buy_day. Take the max profit (or 0 if none positive). Correct but quadratic — won't pass for n=10^5.

approaches.name: Single-Pass Min + Max Profit
approaches.code:
def max_profit(prices):
    min_price = prices[0]
    best = 0
    for p in prices[1:]:
        best = max(best, p - min_price)
        min_price = min(min_price, p)
    return best
approaches.explanation: Walk through prices once. Track the minimum price seen so far. At each day, the max profit if I sold today is current_price - min_price. Track the best across all days. The key: compute profit FIRST using the OLD min, THEN update the min for tomorrow. O(n) time, O(1) space — optimal.

title: Maximum Subarray
summary: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) which has the largest sum and return its sum.

examples.explanation: The subarray [4,-1,2,1] has the largest sum 6.
Single element subarray.
The entire array sums to 23.

test_cases.label: all positives — sum the whole array
all negatives — return the least negative

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Read the problem carefully — 'contiguous' is the key word. Subsequences don't count, only consecutive elements. Restate in your own words. The trick is to recognize this as a DP-friendly problem before you start coding.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the array contain all negative numbers?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Answer is then the LEAST negative single element. This is THE edge case that breaks naive 'best = 0' initialization. Asking this signals you know the gotcha.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I return the sum, or the actual subarray (with indices)?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says sum. Variants ('Maximum Subarray II') ask for the indices or the subarray itself. Always confirm — code structure changes.

clarifying_questions.question: Does 'at least one number' mean the answer can't be the empty subarray (sum 0)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — you must include at least one number. This matters for all-negative inputs: you return the max negative, NOT zero.

clarifying_questions.question: Are integer overflows a concern?
clarifying_questions.why: n=10^5 and |val|=10^4 → worst-case sum is 10^9, fits in int32 (max 2.1×10^9). Worth checking in languages with strict types (Java, C++).

clarifying_questions.question: Should the result use the original indices? Should I preserve input?
clarifying_questions.why: Most variants don't mutate. Confirm — but Kadane's doesn't need mutation anyway.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: State brute force first: try every (i,j) pair as subarray endpoints. O(n²) or O(n³) depending on whether you precompute sums. Then derive Kadane's: 'At each index, I have a choice: extend the previous subarray, or start fresh here. The decision is — extend if the running sum is positive, else start fresh.' This phrasing IS the DP recurrence.

edge_cases.details: [-3,-2,-5,-1] → -1 (the max single element). The most common failure: returning 0 because best = 0 initialization.
[5] → 5. [-3] → -3. Base case for any recurrence.

edge_cases.category: All positive — sum the whole thing
edge_cases.details: [1,2,3] → 6. Kadane's still works: never restart, just keep extending.
[5, -100, 5] → 5. Restart after the -100 because cur+(-100) is far below the next value.
[1,-1,1,-1,1] → 1. Verify Kadane handles oscillation correctly.

common_mistakes: Initializing best = 0 or cur = 0 instead of nums[0]. Breaks all-negatives case (returns 0, wrong).
Forgetting the 'at least one number' constraint — interpreting empty subarray as valid with sum 0.
Off-by-one on the loop (iterating from i=0 vs i=1).
Confusing 'running sum' with 'best sum' — updating the wrong variable when comparing.
Trying to use sliding window — but sliding window requires a non-negative invariant. Doesn't fit here.

faang.introduction: Classic dynamic-programming-style problem. Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum.
faang.introduction_context: Almost universally asked. Tests whether you can derive Kadane's algorithm (the 'extend or restart' decision) or recognize it. Common in 45-min loops and screens.
really_testing: Can you go from O(n²) brute force to O(n) Kadane's by yourself, or only when prompted?
Can you articulate the DP recurrence ('extend the running sum or start fresh')?
Do you initialize correctly for the all-negative case?
Can you trace through a mixed example confidently?
Are you systematic about edge cases?

rubric.criteria: Asked the all-negative question unprompted. States brute force O(n²) without coding it. Derives Kadane's by articulating the 'extend vs restart' decision out loud. Initializes correctly (best = cur = nums[0]). Traces through example. Handles all-negatives in their first attempt. Total time: ~10 min.
Reaches Kadane's after some thought. May need a nudge on the recurrence. Catches the all-negative initialization issue when probed. Code is correct.
Initializes best = 0 and returns 0 for all-negatives. Needed a hint to derive Kadane's. Or stuck at O(n²) prefix-sum approach without seeing the O(n) optimization.
Couldn't derive O(n) even with hints. Can't articulate the 'extend or restart' decision. Returns wrong answer on all-negatives and doesn't recognize the bug.

playbook: Restate the problem aloud, emphasizing 'contiguous'.
Ask the all-negative question. Confirm 'at least one number' means you can't return 0.
State brute force O(n²): try every start/end pair. Don't code it.
Derive Kadane: 'At each index, I choose to extend the running subarray or start fresh. The decision is: if the previous running sum is positive, extending helps; if negative, start fresh.'
State the recurrence: cur = max(x, cur + x); best = max(best, cur). This IS Kadane's.
Walk through the example [-2,1,-3,4,-1,2,1,-5,4] step by step. Show cur and best at each index.
Code carefully: initialize best = cur = nums[0]. Iterate from index 1.
Test on all-negative case [-3,-2,-5,-1] explicitly. Confirm output is -1, not 0.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min for a strong run. Faster if you immediately recognize Kadane's. Common follow-ups: return the subarray (track indices), max product subarray (different — sign flips matter), 2D max submatrix.
dont_do: Don't initialize best = 0 or cur = 0. Breaks all-negatives.
Don't return 0 if no positives found. Violates 'at least one number'.
Don't sort the array. Loses contiguity — fatal.
Don't use a sliding window. Sliding window needs a non-negative invariant; this doesn't have one.
Don't reach for prefix sums first. They give O(n²), not O(n). Kadane is strictly better.
Don't go silent while deriving Kadane. Narrate the 'extend or restart' decision out loud — that's the signal interviewers want.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every starting index, sum forward. Track the best sum encountered. Correct but O(n²) — won't pass n=10^5 in interview time.

approaches.name: Kadane's Algorithm
approaches.explanation: At each index, decide: extend the previous subarray (cur + x) or start fresh from here (x). Take the max — this is the DP recurrence. Track the global best separately. The key insight: if the previous sum is negative, it can only hurt us, so we start fresh.

title: 3Sum
summary: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i, j, k are distinct indices and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. The solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.

examples.explanation: The triplets are -1 + 0 + 1 = 0 and -1 + -1 + 2 = 0. Note: [-1,-1,2] is counted once even though multiple -1s exist.
No three numbers sum to zero.
The only possible triplet sums to zero.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Read the problem carefully — especially 'distinct indices' and 'no duplicate triplets'. Restate it in your own words before coding. Duplicate handling is the entire interview here — it's what separates Strong Hire from Lean No-Hire.

clarifying_questions.question: What does 'no duplicate triplets' mean — distinct by value or by index?
clarifying_questions.why: By value. [-1,-1,2] counts once even if multiple -1s exist in the input. Critical to confirm — interviewers will explicitly say 'no duplicates' and watch whether you remember to dedupe.

clarifying_questions.question: Can I sort the input array?
clarifying_questions.why: Sorting unlocks the O(n²) two-pointer approach. Without it, you're stuck with O(n²) hash-set deduplication that's slower in practice. Most candidates skip this question and burn 5 minutes.

clarifying_questions.question: Are negative numbers allowed? What if all values are positive?
clarifying_questions.why: Negatives are required (otherwise no triplet sums to 0 unless all zeros). If all positive, you can early-exit. Knowing this signals optimization instinct.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of nums?
clarifying_questions.why: n ≤ 3000 means O(n²) = 9M ops, totally fine. If n were 10^5, O(n²) would time out and you'd need a smarter approach (rare for 3Sum).

clarifying_questions.question: What's the expected output format — list of lists, set of tuples?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says list of lists. Ordering within triplets matters for grading. Confirm before assuming.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Start with brute force: three nested loops, O(n³). State it but don't code it — too slow. Then propose: sort the array, then for each index i, use two pointers (left=i+1, right=n-1) to find pairs summing to -nums[i]. This makes it O(n²) plus the sort. The key insight: sorting unlocks the two-pointer pattern.

edge_cases.details: [0,0,0] → [[0,0,0]]. Just one triplet, all zeros. Make sure your dup-skip doesn't accidentally remove this.
Constraint says n ≥ 3, but defensive code returns []. Show you considered it.
Can't sum to zero unless all zeros. Optional early exit after sorting.
[-1,-1,-1,-1,2,2,2] — the two-pointer dup-skip is critical. Both the outer i AND inner left/right must skip duplicates.
Return []. Constraint forbids this but be safe.

common_mistakes: Not skipping duplicates on the outer i — produces duplicate triplets in output.
Not skipping duplicates on inner left/right after finding a triplet — produces e.g. [-1,-1,2] twice when input has [-1,-1,-1,2].
Using a hash set to dedupe instead of sorted skip — works but O(n²) space and slower in practice. Signals weaker pattern recognition.
Off-by-one on initial left/right pointers (should be i+1 and n-1).
Modifying the input array (sorting) without asking permission first.

faang.introduction: OK, you've done Two Sum. Let's level up. Given an integer array, find all unique triplets that sum to zero. No duplicate triplets allowed.
faang.introduction_context: Almost always comes after Two Sum or as a standalone medium. Tests pattern recognition (sort + two-pointer) and the dup-skip detail — the SINGLE thing that fails most candidates.
really_testing: Can you recognize the 'sorted array + two pointers' pattern as a generalization of Two Sum?
Can you handle duplicate-skipping correctly on first attempt?
Do you propose the sort+two-pointer approach over hash-set dedup (the inferior alternative)?
Are you systematic about edge cases (empty, all zeros, all negatives)?

rubric.criteria: Asked 'can I sort?' unprompted. Stated brute force O(n³) without coding it. Implemented sort + two-pointer with dup-skip on BOTH outer i and inner left/right correctly on first try. Traced through the duplicates example. Total time: ~15 min.
Reached the optimal O(n²) sort + two-pointer. May have missed the inner dup-skip initially but caught it after a nudge or while testing. Clean code.

Reached the optimal algorithm but used a hash set to dedupe (slower, signals weak pattern recognition). Or got the algorithm right but produced duplicate triplets in output and didn't notice until interviewer pointed it out.
Stuck at O(n³) brute force. Couldn't see how to leverage sorting. Got confused by duplicate handling. Produced wrong output and couldn't debug.

playbook: Restate aloud, emphasizing 'unique triplets' and 'distinct indices'.
Ask: 'Can I sort the input?' — this is THE question. If interviewer says no, you're heading toward hash-set dedup (slower). Most will say yes.
Propose brute force out loud: 'Three nested loops, O(n³). Won't pass for n=3000.' Don't code it.
State the optimization: 'Sort the array O(n log n), then for each i, use two pointers left=i+1, right=n-1 to find pairs summing to -nums[i]. Total O(n²).'
Walk through the example [-1,0,1,2,-1,-4] after sorting → [-4,-1,-1,0,1,2]. Show two-pointer movement and dup-skip on i.
Code: outer loop with `if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue` (skip dup i). Inner two-pointer with dup-skip on left AND right after each match.
Test on the duplicates example. Confirm output has each triplet exactly once.
Mention edge cases: all zeros [[0,0,0]] (test that the code handles 0+0+0), all negatives (no answer, returns []).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-20 min for a strong run. Faster if you nail dup-skip on first try. The follow-up is often '4Sum' or 'find all triplets with sum closest to target'.
dont_do: Don't use a hash set to dedupe — signals weak pattern recognition. Sort + skip is the textbook answer.
Don't forget to skip duplicates on the OUTER i. THE most common 3Sum bug.
Don't forget to skip duplicates on inner left/right AFTER finding a triplet. Subtle but critical.
Don't code brute force first — interviewers know it's O(n³). Just state and move on.
Don't sort the input without asking — some interviews care about input immutability.
Don't use return early on first found triplet — find ALL of them.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Three nested loops, check every possible triplet. Use a set of sorted tuples to dedupe. Correct but O(n³) — won't pass n=3000 in interview time limits.

approaches.name: Sort + Two Pointers
approaches.space: O(1) extra (or O(n) if sort is not in-place)
approaches.explanation: Sort the array first. For each index i, use two pointers (left=i+1, right=n-1) and squeeze them toward each other based on whether their sum with nums[i] is too small or too large. The duplicate-skip logic (on outer i AND on inner left/right after finding a triplet) is what prevents duplicate triplets in the output. The sorted order makes this cleaner than hash-set dedup.

title: Container With Most Water
summary: You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the ith line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]). Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water. Return the maximum amount of water a container can store.

examples.explanation: Lines at indices 1 (height 8) and 8 (height 7) — width = 7, min height = 7, area = 49.
Width = 1, height = 1, area = 1.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Visualize the heights as vertical lines on a chart. Water level between two lines is limited by the SHORTER one. Area = min(h[i], h[j]) * (j - i). Brute force tries every pair — O(n²). Two-pointer approach starts at both ends and squeezes inward.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the formula for water held between two lines?
clarifying_questions.why: min(heights) × width. Confirm before coding — interviewers want to hear you state it. The min is because the taller line spills over the shorter one.

clarifying_questions.question: Can heights be zero?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per constraints (0 <= h <= 10^4). A zero-height line holds no water on its own but doesn't prevent it being the 'taller' partner — though min(0, anything) = 0, so it never contributes.

clarifying_questions.question: How big can n be?
clarifying_questions.why: 10^5. O(n²) too slow. Must reach O(n).

clarifying_questions.question: Are heights bounded?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 10^4. Width up to 10^5. Max area = 10^9, fits in int32.

clarifying_questions.question: Do indices count from 0 or 1 for the width calculation?
clarifying_questions.why: Doesn't matter for the answer (width is right-left), but worth confirming if the problem statement is ambiguous.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Start with brute force: try every pair, compute area, track max. O(n²). State this but DON'T code it. Now derive two-pointer: place L at 0, R at n-1. Compute the area. Then ask: 'Which pointer should I move inward?' Answer: always move the SMALLER height. Why? The shorter line is the bottleneck. Moving the taller one inward can only decrease the width without raising the bottleneck — can NEVER improve. Moving the shorter one inward might find a taller line that raises the bottleneck.

edge_cases.details: [3,3] → area = 3 (width 1, height 3). Confirm the simplest case.
[4,3,2,1,4] → 16 (width 4, min height 4). Two-pointer immediately gives this because L=0, R=4 are both 4.
[1,2,3,4,5] → 4 (width 4, min(1,5)=1, area 4). The shorter end is the bottleneck.
[0,0,0,0] → 0. Confirm the min logic returns 0.
[1,8,1,1,8,1] → 24 (the two 8s, width 3). Two-pointer finds this.

common_mistakes: Moving BOTH pointers each step — incorrect; can skip optimal pairs.
Moving the LARGER pointer instead of the smaller — provably can never improve.
Greedily picking the two highest lines — misses cases where width matters more.
Off-by-one on width: it's right - left, NOT right - left + 1 (we're measuring distance, not count).
Stopping early when areas seem to decrease — the max may still be ahead.

faang.introduction: Two-pointers classic. Given heights as vertical lines, find two that hold the most water.
faang.introduction_context: Often paired with 3Sum as a two-pointer essentials check. Tests both pattern recognition AND the greedy proof (why move the smaller pointer).
really_testing: Recognition of the two-pointer-from-ends pattern.
Can you ARTICULATE why moving the smaller pointer is correct?
Comfort with the area formula (width × min, not max).
Edge case handling (zeros, equal heights, mountain shapes).

rubric.criteria: States brute force without coding. Proposes two-pointer immediately. Articulates the greedy proof: 'moving the taller inward can only decrease width without raising the bottleneck, so we should always move the shorter.' Codes cleanly. ~10 min.
Reaches two-pointer with some derivation. Code correct. May not fully justify the greedy choice without prompting.
Stuck at O(n²). Or implements two-pointer but moves the wrong pointer. Or computes area with width = right - left + 1.
Can't reach O(n). Confused about which pointer to move. Wrong area formula.

playbook: Restate: 'Find two lines that hold the most water'.
State the area formula aloud: width × min(heights). Confirm this is correct.
Brute force out loud: every pair, O(n²). Don't code it.
Propose two pointers from both ends.

BEFORE coding, articulate the greedy proof: 'Moving the smaller pointer is correct because the smaller height is the bottleneck. The taller line is wasted height. Moving the taller inward can only shrink the width while keeping the same bottleneck, so it can never improve. Moving the shorter inward might find a taller new partner that raises the bottleneck.'
Code: L=0, R=n-1, best=0. While L<R: area = min * width; update best; move whichever pointer has the smaller height.
Walk through [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]: L=0(1), R=8(7), area=8. Move L (1<7). L=1(8), area = min(8,7)*7=49 — found it. Continue to verify no better pair exists.
Test on [4,3,2,1,4] → confirm 16.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. The greedy proof articulation is the senior signal. Follow-ups: trapping rain water (harder variant), max subarray (DP).
dont_do: Don't greedily pick the two highest lines — width matters.
Don't move both pointers each step.
Don't move the LARGER pointer — it provably can't improve.
Don't forget to articulate WHY moving the smaller pointer is correct. Without the proof, you sound like you memorized it.
Don't use width = right - left + 1 — width is the DISTANCE between lines, not the inclusive index count.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every pair of lines. For each, compute area = min(heights) × width. Track the best. Quadratic — won't pass n=10^5.

approaches.name: Two Pointers
approaches.explanation: Start with pointers at both ends. The area is bottlenecked by the shorter line. Greedy insight: moving the taller pointer inward can ONLY decrease width without raising the bottleneck — can never improve. Moving the shorter pointer inward MIGHT find a taller new partner that raises the bottleneck. So always move the smaller. Each step eliminates one line from consideration (the shorter one paired with the longer one is now off the table). O(n) total.

title: Product of Array Except Self
summary: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i]. The algorithm must run in O(n) time and without using the division operation.
constraints: The product of any prefix or suffix of nums is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer.
You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time and without using the division operation.

examples.explanation: Because there's a 0 in the array, every answer position except the one occupied BY the 0 gets multiplied by 0. answer[2] = (-1)*1*(-3)*3 = 9 (the only nonzero result).

test_cases.label: with a zero (only one nonzero result)

interview_tips.talk_through_it: The problem forbids division — that's the entire interview. Naive 'total product / nums[i]' is trivial but disallowed, AND fails when there's a zero. The trick: compute prefix products (everything to the left of i) and suffix products (everything to the right of i). Answer at position i is prefix[i] × suffix[i].

clarifying_questions.question: Confirm — no division operation allowed?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec explicitly forbids it. Asking signals you read the constraints. Division also fails on zeros, so it's the wrong tool anyway.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the array contain zeros? Negatives?
clarifying_questions.why: Both possible per constraints. Zeros are the killer — division-based approaches fail. Negatives flip signs but multiplication handles them naturally.

clarifying_questions.question: Output space — can I allocate a new array?
clarifying_questions.why: Always, but the senior signal is the O(1)-extra-space variant where the output array IS the workspace. Confirm the output array isn't counted as 'extra' space.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of n?
clarifying_questions.why: n up to 10^5. O(n) is required. O(n²) brute force times out.

clarifying_questions.question: Are values bounded for overflow?
clarifying_questions.why: Per spec, products fit in 32-bit int. So you don't need bignum handling.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Brute force: for each i, multiply all elements except nums[i]. O(n²). Division trick: compute total product, then total/nums[i] — BUT forbidden, AND fails on zeros. The clean solution: build prefix products (product of everything before i) and suffix products (product of everything after i). answer[i] = prefix[i] × suffix[i]. Two passes = O(n) time. Two arrays = O(n) extra space. Optimization: reuse the output array — first pass fills it with prefix products; second pass walks right-to-left multiplying by a running suffix value. O(1) extra space.

edge_cases.details: [1,2,0,4] → [0, 0, 8, 0]. Only the position where the zero is gets the nonzero product.
[1,0,2,0] → [0, 0, 0, 0]. Two zeros means every position has at least one zero in the product.
[2,3] → [3, 2]. Simplest case — each element is just the other.

common_mistakes: Using division — explicitly forbidden by the problem.
Forgetting to handle zeros if you DO try division (you'd need to special-case 'count of zeros').
Allocating three arrays (prefix, suffix, output) when two suffice — or one with two passes.
Off-by-one in the prefix/suffix indices — prefix[i] should be product of nums[0..i-1] (not including i).
Misreading the spec and including nums[i] in the product for position i.

faang.introduction: Product of array except self. Given nums, return an array where answer[i] is the product of every element except nums[i]. No division allowed. O(n) time required.
faang.introduction_context: Classic prefix/suffix pattern. The 'no division' constraint is the whole point — forces you into the elegant approach. The O(1)-extra-space optimization separates Hire from Strong Hire.
really_testing: Recognition of the prefix/suffix pattern.
Handling zeros gracefully (the division trap).
Space optimization: reusing the output array for O(1) extra space.
Following constraints carefully (no division, O(n) time).

rubric.criteria: Notices 'no division' and 'O(n)' upfront. Proposes prefix/suffix arrays. Implements with two arrays, then optimizes to O(1) extra by reusing output. Handles zeros without special-casing. ~12 min.
Reaches the two-array prefix/suffix approach. Correct. May not optimize to O(1) space without prompting.
Proposes division and gets bitten by the no-division constraint. Or stuck at O(n²) brute force.
Couldn't reach O(n). Or violates 'no division' even after being told.

playbook: Restate the problem. Emphasize 'no division' and 'O(n) time'.
Ask about zeros and whether output array counts as extra space.
State brute force: nested loop, O(n²). Don't code.
State the division shortcut, then explain why it fails: forbidden AND fails on zeros.
Derive prefix/suffix: 'answer[i] = product_before_i × product_after_i. Build two arrays in O(n) each.'
Walk through [1,2,3,4]: prefix = [1,1,2,6], suffix = [24,12,4,1]. answer = element-wise product.
Code two-array version first (cleaner to understand).
Optimize: 'Can I reuse the output array? First pass: fill output with prefix products. Second pass: walk right-to-left maintaining a running suffix value, multiply each output[i] by it.'
Code O(1)-extra-space version.
Test on the zero example to confirm correctness.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-15 min. Optimization to O(1) space is the senior signal. Common follow-up: 'What if division WAS allowed and there were zeros?' (Count zeros, special-case).
dont_do: Don't propose division as the solution. Even if you think it's clever, the spec explicitly bans it AND it fails on zeros.
Don't special-case zeros if you use prefix/suffix — the multiplication handles them naturally.
Don't allocate three arrays when two (or one + scalar) suffice.
Don't get confused about indices: prefix[i] is product of [0..i-1] (everything BEFORE i, exclusive).
Don't include nums[i] in the product for position i. Read carefully.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each position, multiply all other elements. Quadratic — won't pass n=10^5.

approaches.name: Prefix and Suffix Arrays
approaches.explanation: Build two arrays. prefix[i] = product of nums[0..i-1] (everything before i). suffix[i] = product of nums[i+1..n-1] (everything after i). answer[i] = prefix[i] × suffix[i]. Two linear passes, no division, handles zeros naturally.

approaches.name: O(1) Extra Space (reuse output)
approaches.code:
def product_except_self(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    result = [1] * n
    # First pass: result[i] = prefix product (everything before i)
    for i in range(1, n):
        result[i] = result[i-1] * nums[i-1]
    # Second pass: walk right-to-left, multiply by running suffix product
    suffix = 1
    for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
        result[i] *= suffix
        suffix *= nums[i]
    return result
approaches.explanation: The senior optimization. First pass fills result[] with prefix products. Second pass walks right-to-left, maintaining a single scalar 'suffix' that tracks the product of everything after position i. Multiply each result[i] by this suffix. Total: two passes, O(n) time, O(1) extra space (the output array doesn't count toward 'extra').

title: Rotate Array
summary: Given an integer array nums, rotate the array to the right by k steps, where k is non-negative. The classic asks for an in-place O(1) extra space solution.
starter_code:
def rotate(nums, k):
    # Rotate nums to the right by k steps.
    # Mutate nums in-place AND return it (so the test runner can compare).
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two obvious ideas (extra array, or rotate-one-step-k-times) are correct but suboptimal. The classic answer is the three-reverse trick: reverse the whole array, then reverse the first k and last n-k. O(n) time, O(1) extra space, in-place. The interviewer is specifically watching for this insight.

clarifying_questions.question: Can k be larger than the array length?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — k can be up to 10^5 while nums can be as short as 1. Must reduce k = k % n at the start or you'll over-rotate.

clarifying_questions.question: Is k always non-negative?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, yes. But worth confirming — a negative k means rotate left. If allowed, handle k = ((k % n) + n) % n.

clarifying_questions.question: Must the rotation be in-place?
clarifying_questions.why: This is THE point of the problem. LC explicitly asks for O(1) extra space as a follow-up. If you don't ask, the interviewer will.

clarifying_questions.question: Should the function return nums or mutate it?
clarifying_questions.why: LC's signature mutates in-place and returns None. For testability here, mutate AND return. Confirms understanding of Python list semantics.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the array guaranteed non-empty?
clarifying_questions.why: Constraints say length >= 1. Still, guarding against empty arrays is cheap insurance.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: The naive O(n*k) of rotating one step at a time will TLE. O(n) extra space (build new array) is correct but fails the follow-up. The trick: reversing the whole array puts what should be the front at the back, then reversing each half puts them in the right internal order. Draw it out: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7] k=3 → reverse all: [7,6,5,4,3,2,1] → reverse first 3: [5,6,7,4,3,2,1] → reverse last 4: [5,6,7,1,2,3,4]. Done.

edge_cases.details: No rotation. Reverse trick still works (reverses then reverses back). But k = k % n catches it first.
Full rotation = identity. k % n = 0 — same as the k=0 case.
Must reduce. k = 5, n = 2 → effective k = 1. Forgetting this is the #1 bug.
Any k leaves it unchanged. Guards trivially.
Values don't matter to the algorithm — only positions. But confirms you understand the problem is positional.

common_mistakes: Not reducing k modulo n — over-rotates when k > n.
Off-by-one in the split index (k vs k-1, n-k vs n-k-1).
Using slice assignment (nums = nums[-k:] + nums[:-k]) — looks in-place but rebinds the local name; the caller's list is unchanged.
Reversing only one of the two halves — produces a scrambled result.
Using nums[:] = ... slice trick — correct but uses O(n) extra space during the slice, defeating the follow-up.

faang.introduction: Array manipulation classic. The interviewer is hunting for the three-reverse trick specifically — it's the textbook O(1)-space rotation algorithm.
faang.introduction_context: Shows up in early/mid rounds at Amazon, Meta, Microsoft. Often paired with 'now do it in O(1) extra space' as a follow-up if you give the extra-array solution first.
really_testing: Whether you ask 'can k > n?' before coding (off-by-one detector).
Whether you know the three-reverse trick (or can derive it).
Comfort writing in-place index manipulation cleanly.
Distinguishing 'in-place' (mutate the caller's list) from 'rebind the local name'.

rubric.criteria: Asks about k > n. Proposes three-reverse with clear reasoning. Codes cleanly in 5-8 min. Walks through k=3 example on a 7-element array. Handles all edge cases.
Solves with extra array first, then derives O(1) version when prompted. Handles k > n. Correct final code.
Gets the extra-array solution but cannot derive in-place even with hints. Or buggy reverse indices. Doesn't handle k > n.
Suggests O(n*k) one-step rotation as the final answer. Or cannot produce any correct solution.

playbook: Restate: 'rotate right by k, in-place, O(1) extra space'.
Clarifying questions: k > n? In-place required?
First pass: 'I could use an extra array — nums[(i+k) % n] = nums[i]. Want me to write that, or aim for the in-place version directly?'
Three-reverse: 'Reverse the whole array, then reverse [0..k-1], then reverse [k..n-1].' Draw it.
Code: def rotate(nums, k): n = len(nums); k %= n; nums.reverse(); reverse(nums, 0, k-1); reverse(nums, k, n-1).
Edge: k=0 — k%=n handles it. Single element — trivially correct.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 7-10 min. Follow-ups: 'rotate left' (mirror with k = n - k), 'rotate matrix 90°' (Rotate Image, LC 48).
dont_do: Don't write nums = nums[-k:] + nums[:-k] — rebinds the local; caller sees no change.
Don't forget k %= n — huge k will index out of bounds or do extra work.
Don't propose the cyclic-replacement algorithm as your first answer — correct but harder to code under pressure; save it as a follow-up.
Don't skip drawing the example — reverse indices are the most error-prone part.
Don't use Python's built-in slicing as your 'in-place' answer — it's O(n) extra space.

approaches.name: Extra Array
approaches.explanation: Build a fresh array where each element from nums lands at index (i + k) % n. Then copy it back into nums in-place. Correct and easy to reason about — but uses O(n) extra space, which fails the follow-up.

approaches.name: Three Reverses (in-place)
approaches.code:
def rotate(nums, k):
    n = len(nums)
    k %= n
    
    def reverse(lo, hi):
        while lo < hi:
            nums[lo], nums[hi] = nums[hi], nums[lo]
            lo += 1
            hi -= 1
    
    reverse(0, n - 1)        # reverse whole array
    reverse(0, k - 1)        # reverse first k
    reverse(k, n - 1)        # reverse last n-k
    return nums
approaches.explanation: The textbook in-place rotation. Reversing the whole array makes the last k elements appear first — but in reverse order. The two follow-up reverses fix each segment's internal order. Each element is swapped at most twice, so the total work is O(n) with O(1) extra memory. This is the answer the interviewer is looking for.

approaches.name: Cyclic Replacement
approaches.code:
def rotate(nums, k):
    n = len(nums)
    k %= n
    count = 0
    start = 0
    while count < n:
        current = start
        prev = nums[start]
        while True:
            next_idx = (current + k) % n
            nums[next_idx], prev = prev, nums[next_idx]
            current = next_idx
            count += 1
            if current == start:
                break
        start += 1
    return nums
approaches.explanation: Walk cycles in the permutation i → (i + k) % n, swapping along the way. Hits each element exactly once. Conceptually elegant but harder to get right under interview pressure — prefer three-reverse unless explicitly asked for something different.

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
summary: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time.

examples.explanation: The longest consecutive sequence is [1,2,3,4]. Length 4.
The longest consecutive sequence is [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8]. Length 9.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: The 'must be O(n)' constraint rules out sort. The trick: put nums in a hash set, then walk only from SEQUENCE STARTS — numbers where (num - 1) is NOT in the set. From each start, count forward (num+1, num+2, ...) while they're in the set. This guarantees O(n) total because each number is visited at most twice (once as a candidate start, once during a forward walk).

clarifying_questions.question: Does the algorithm need to be strictly O(n)?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says yes. That rules out sort O(n log n). Confirms you need the hash-set-with-start-detection trick.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the array have duplicates?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes per constraints. Duplicates don't affect the longest sequence (the set dedupes naturally). Confirm so you handle [5,5,5,5] correctly.

clarifying_questions.question: Can values be negative or zero?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, yes (-10^9 to 10^9). Hash works fine for any int. Worth confirming you're not assuming positives.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty array?
clarifying_questions.why: Returns 0. Trivial but worth confirming.

clarifying_questions.question: Single-element array?
clarifying_questions.why: Returns 1. The sequence of length 1 is just that element.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Sort and scan is O(n log n) — too slow per spec. Naive set + walk-forward from EVERY number is O(n²) worst case (input [1,2,3,...,n] makes you walk from each number). The fix: only walk forward from numbers that DON'T have a predecessor in the set. These are sequence starts. Walking forward from a start is O(length of sequence). Across all starts, the total work is exactly the sum of all sequence lengths = n. So O(n).

edge_cases.details: [] → 0. Set is empty, no iterations, max stays at 0.
[5] → 1. {5} has no predecessor (4 not in set), so 5 is a start. Walk forward: 6 not in set. Length 1.
[5,5,5,5] → 1. Set is {5}. Same as single element case.
[1,2,3,4,5] → 5. Only 1 is a start. Walk forward through 2,3,4,5. Length 5. Numbers 2,3,4,5 are NOT starts (their predecessors are in set), so skipped.

common_mistakes: Walking forward from every number → O(n²) worst case. The start-detection check is what makes it O(n).
Forgetting to check if num is a start (num-1 not in set) before walking forward.
Sorting the array — works correctly but fails the O(n) spec requirement.
Using a list for set membership — O(n) lookup makes the whole thing O(n²).
Off-by-one when counting the sequence length (e.g., starting count at 0 instead of 1).

faang.introduction: Longest consecutive sequence. Given an unsorted array, find the longest run of consecutive integers — must be O(n).
faang.introduction_context: The 'must be O(n)' constraint is the entire interview. Sorting is the obvious O(n log n) answer; the trick is the hash-set-with-start-detection that brings it to O(n). Classic FAANG question.
really_testing: Reading constraints — recognizing the O(n) hint that rules out sort.
Pattern recognition: 'consecutive integers' → 'hash set + neighbor lookup'.
The cleverness moment: realizing naive set-walk is O(n²), then finding the start-detection fix.
Articulating WHY start-detection achieves O(n) (each number visited at most twice).

rubric.criteria: Notes 'must be O(n)' immediately. Proposes hash set with start-detection. Articulates the amortized analysis (each number walked at most twice). Codes cleanly. ~10 min.
Reaches set-based approach. Initial version may be naive O(n²); catches the issue after testing or hint. Final version is O(n).
Sorts the array (fails O(n) spec). Or naive set walk from every number without realizing it's O(n²).
Can't reach O(n) even with hints. Doesn't recognize the hash set pattern.

playbook: Restate the problem. Highlight 'must be O(n)' — that's the constraint that shapes the approach.
State sort and scan: O(n log n), correct but fails the spec.
State naive set + walk forward from every number — works but O(n²) worst case on [1,2,3,...,n].
Derive the fix: 'Only walk forward from sequence STARTS — numbers where num-1 is NOT in the set.' This guarantees each number is touched at most twice.
Code: put nums in set. For each num in set: if num-1 not in set, walk forward (num+1, num+2, ...) while in set, track length. Update max.
Test on [100,4,200,1,3,2]: starts are 100, 1, 200. Walks produce lengths 1, 4, 1. Max = 4.
Articulate the O(n) argument: 'Each number is walked through at most twice — once as a potential start (constant check), once during a forward walk from its sequence's start.'
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. The start-detection trick is the interview moment. Common follow-ups: longest consecutive sequence in a stream, or 2D version (longest path in grid).
dont_do: Don't sort when the spec says O(n). Read constraints carefully.
Don't walk forward from every number — O(n²) on the right input.
Don't use a list for set operations — O(n) lookup defeats the algorithm.
Don't forget to skip numbers that aren't sequence starts.
Don't return 0 instead of 1 for a single-element input — off-by-one.

approaches.name: Sort and Scan
approaches.space: O(1) or O(n) depending on sort
approaches.explanation: Sort the (deduped) array, then scan looking for runs of consecutive integers. Correct but O(n log n) — fails the spec's O(n) requirement.

approaches.name: Hash Set with Start-Detection
approaches.explanation: Put nums in a set for O(1) lookups. For each unique number, check if it's a sequence start (num-1 not in set). If yes, walk forward counting consecutive numbers. The amortized cost is O(n): each number is touched at most twice — once when checked as a candidate start, once when walked through during forward scan from its sequence's start. Numbers that aren't starts are dismissed in O(1).

title: Trapping Rain Water
summary: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.

examples.explanation: The elevation map traps 6 units of water in the dips between bars.
Water trapped: at index 1 (2 units), index 2 (3 units), index 3 (2 units), index 4 (2 units) = 9.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Key insight: water at position i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]. Where maxLeft is the tallest bar to the LEFT of i (inclusive) and maxRight is the tallest to the RIGHT. Brute force computes these maxes per-position in O(n²). Precomputed prefix/suffix-max arrays make it O(n) time + O(n) space. The senior answer is the two-pointer O(1) space approach.

clarifying_questions.question: How is water trapped — is it that 'water sits in dips bounded by taller bars on both sides'?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Confirms the model. Without this clarification, candidates sometimes confuse this with 'sum of heights' or other interpretations.

clarifying_questions.question: Can heights be zero?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes (0 <= h). A zero-height position can still trap water if both sides have taller bars.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of n?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 2×10^4. O(n²) brute force is borderline (4×10^8 ops). O(n) is safer.

clarifying_questions.question: Are there overflow concerns on the total?
clarifying_questions.why: Max heights 10^5, n=2×10^4, so max water ≤ 10^5 × 2×10^4 = 2×10^9. Just over int32 range. Worth flagging if language is type-strict.

clarifying_questions.question: Single-element or empty array — what's the answer?
clarifying_questions.why: 0 — can't trap water with fewer than 3 bars (need walls on both sides).

interview_tips.think_out_loud: State the formula: water_at_i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i] (clamped to ≥ 0). Brute force: for each i, scan left and right for the max. O(n²). Better: precompute maxLeft[] and maxRight[] arrays in two passes. O(n) time, O(n) space. Best: two pointers. Maintain leftMax and rightMax scalars. Move whichever pointer has the SMALLER side max — because the smaller side determines the water level (the larger side has 'extra' height that doesn't affect the bottleneck).

edge_cases.details: [0], [5], [5,5] → 0. Need at least 3 bars to trap any water.
[1,2,3,4,5] → 0. No dip. Strictly increasing or decreasing traps nothing.
[3,0,0,3] → 6. Both walls are 3, dip is 2 wide, water level is 3, total = 3 × 2 = 6.
[100, 1, 100] → 99. Single low position bounded by tall walls.
[4,2,0,3,2,5] → 9. Right wall (5) is taller than left wall (4). Water level on the left side is limited by 4, not 5.

common_mistakes: Forgetting that the height of the current bar SUBTRACTS from the water level (water = min - height, not just min).
Adding negative water — when current height exceeds the smaller max, water contribution is 0, not negative.
Two-pointer: moving the WRONG side (always move the smaller-max side, not the smaller-current-height side).
Trying to use a single-pass scan without thinking about left/right maxes — fundamentally wrong.
Off-by-one on the maxLeft/maxRight arrays (e.g., including height[i] in maxLeft[i] when it shouldn't be).

faang.introduction: Hard. Given an elevation map of bar heights, compute the total water trapped after rain.
faang.introduction_context: Classic FAANG hard. The two-pointer O(1) space approach separates strong candidates from average. Often follows Container With Most Water — both are about pointers from both ends, but Trapping Rain Water is harder because you're computing cumulative water, not max area.
really_testing: Can you derive the water_at_i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i] formula?
Can you reach the prefix/suffix max O(n) solution?
Can you derive the two-pointer O(1) space optimization?
Can you ARTICULATE why moving the smaller-max side is correct?

rubric.criteria: Derives the water formula. Reaches two-pointer O(1) space. Articulates the 'move the smaller side' proof. Tests on edge cases. ~20 min.
Reaches prefix/suffix max approach in O(n) space. Recognizes the two-pointer optimization with a nudge.
Stuck at O(n²) brute force. Or buggy formula (e.g., water = max instead of min).
Can't derive the water formula. Doesn't recognize the maxes-from-both-sides insight.

playbook: Restate. Confirm 'water at each position depends on the tallest bars to its left and right'.
Derive the formula aloud: water_at_i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i], clamped to ≥ 0.
State brute force O(n²): for each i, scan both sides for the max. Don't code it.
Propose prefix/suffix max arrays. O(n) time, O(n) space. Walk through example.
Optimize to two pointers. Articulate: 'Maintain leftMax and rightMax scalars. Move whichever pointer has the smaller max — because the smaller side is the bottleneck for the current position'.

Code carefully. Initialize l=0, r=n-1, leftMax=0, rightMax=0. While l<r: if height[l]<height[r], it means leftMax is potentially the bottleneck; update leftMax with height[l], add (leftMax-height[l]) to total, advance l. Else similarly for right.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-20 min. This is a hard problem — getting to the O(n) prefix/suffix is already a Hire. Two-pointer O(1) is Strong Hire territory.
dont_do: Don't use water = max - height. It's MIN of the two sides, not max.
Don't subtract height[i] from the larger max — you'd get incorrect water at positions where the smaller side is the bottleneck.
Don't move both pointers each step in the two-pointer approach.
Don't move the pointer with the smaller CURRENT HEIGHT — move the one with the smaller MAX-SO-FAR.
Don't add negative water when height[i] exceeds the smaller max — clamp to 0.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.code:
def trap(height):
    n = len(height)
    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        left_max = max(height[:i+1])
        right_max = max(height[i:])
        total += max(0, min(left_max, right_max) - height[i])
    return total
approaches.explanation: For each position, scan left and right to find the maxes. Water at i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i] (clamped to ≥ 0). Quadratic — won't scale.

approaches.name: Prefix and Suffix Max Arrays
approaches.explanation: Precompute the running maxes from both directions in two passes. Then for each position, water = min(maxes) - height[i], clamped. Three linear passes total. O(n) time, O(n) extra space.

approaches.name: Two Pointers (O(1) space)
approaches.explanation: Maintain left_max and right_max as running scalars. At each step, compare height[left] and height[right]. The SMALLER side guarantees its max is the bottleneck for the current position (because the other side has a larger barrier somewhere to its outside). Update that side's max if the current height beats it; otherwise, add (max - height) water. Move that side's pointer inward. O(n) time, O(1) extra space.

title: First Missing Positive
summary: Given an unsorted integer array nums, return the smallest positive integer that is not present. You must solve in O(n) time and use O(1) auxiliary space.

examples.explanation: The numbers in the range [1,2] are all in the array. 3 is missing.
1 is present, 2 is not. Negatives and large values don't matter.
1 isn't in the array. Anything missing from [1..n] is a candidate, smallest wins.

starter_code:
def firstMissingPositive(nums):
    # Return the smallest positive integer not in nums.
    # Aim for O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Key insight: the answer is always between 1 and n+1 (inclusive). Anything outside [1..n] is irrelevant. So treat the array as its own hash table — for each number x in [1..n], place it at index x-1. After this 'cyclic sort' pass, the first index i where nums[i] != i+1 is the answer; if all match, the answer is n+1.

clarifying_questions.question: Is it really O(1) extra space, or can I use O(n)?
clarifying_questions.why: The O(n)-space solution (use a set) is trivial. The whole point of this problem is the in-place answer. If the interviewer says O(n) space is fine, you can write the easy version, but they're almost certainly looking for the harder one.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the array be mutated?
clarifying_questions.why: The cyclic-sort solution mutates the array. If 'must not modify input' is a constraint, fall back to using sign manipulation (negate nums[x-1] to mark presence) — still O(1) space, but uglier.

clarifying_questions.question: Are there duplicates?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes, possibly. The cyclic sort handles them — if nums[i] points to a slot already correct, just skip.

clarifying_questions.question: Negative numbers and zeros — do they count?
clarifying_questions.why: No — the answer is the smallest POSITIVE missing. Negatives, zeros, and values > n can be ignored entirely.

clarifying_questions.question: Can n be very large?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 10^5. O(n*n) brute force passes the spec letter but fails the time bound. Worth confirming bounds to justify the O(n) push.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Three tiers: easy O(n)-space (use a set or boolean array, check 1, 2, ... until missing); medium O(n log n)-time (sort, then scan); hard O(n)/O(1) (cyclic sort or sign marking). For the hard version: since the answer must be in [1..n+1], you only care about values in that range. Cyclic-sort places each in-range value x at index x-1. Then scan: first mismatch wins.

edge_cases.details: If [1..n] are all present, the answer is n+1. e.g., [1,2,3] → 4.
[1] → 2. [5] → 1. [-7] → 1. The rule 'between 1 and n+1' still applies.
Nothing is in [1..n] so cyclic sort does nothing; answer is 1.
[1,1] → 2. When you go to swap and the target slot already holds the right value, skip (else you'd loop forever).
Values < 1 or > n: leave them in place. They're irrelevant to the answer.

common_mistakes: Infinite loop in cyclic sort: not checking nums[nums[i]-1] != nums[i] before swapping leaves duplicates spinning forever.
Off-by-one on the 'answer is n+1' case — forgetting to return n+1 when all slots are filled.
Including 0 or negatives in the swap logic — they have no valid slot.
Confusing index x-1 (where x belongs) with index x (off by one).
Using sorted() and claiming it's O(n) — it's O(n log n).

faang.introduction: The famous 'use the array as its own hash table' problem. Demands the cyclic-sort or sign-marking trick to hit O(n)/O(1).
faang.introduction_context: Asked at Google, Microsoft, Stripe. Often a follow-up to easier array problems. The interviewer wants to see whether you can derive a non-obvious in-place algorithm under pressure.
really_testing: Whether you recognize 'answer must be in [1..n+1]' immediately.
Treating the array as a hash table indexed by value.
Avoiding the infinite-loop trap when handling duplicates.
Trading off difficulty: walk the interviewer up from O(n)-space to O(1)-space.

rubric.criteria: States the [1..n+1] insight immediately. Proposes cyclic sort. Codes the swap loop with the right termination check. Handles duplicates. 12-15 min.
Solves with O(n) extra space (set or boolean array). When prompted, derives O(1)-space with hints.
Infinite loop in cyclic sort. Misses the n+1 case. Or needs heavy hand-holding to write the O(1) version.
Suggests sorting (O(n log n)) and defends it as optimal. Or can't formulate any working algorithm.

playbook: Restate: 'smallest positive missing from nums, in O(n) time and O(1) space'.
Insight: 'the answer is in [1..n+1]. Anything outside that range is irrelevant.'
Pitch: 'use the array as a hash table — place value x at index x-1'.
Code cyclic sort: for i in range(n): while 1 <= nums[i] <= n and nums[nums[i]-1] != nums[i]: swap nums[i] with nums[nums[i]-1].
Scan: for i in range(n): if nums[i] != i+1: return i+1. Else return n+1.
Walk [3,4,-1,1]: after sort → [1, -1, 3, 4]. Mismatch at i=1 (expect 2). Return 2.
Edge: [1,1] — second 1 tries to swap into slot 0 which is already 1; the != check prevents the infinite loop.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Follow-up: 'find ALL missing positives' (apply the same cyclic sort, then collect every mismatch index).
dont_do: Don't sort the array — that's O(n log n) and trivially defeats the point.
Don't forget the != check in the while loop — infinite loop on duplicates.
Don't include 0 or negatives in the swap target — there's no valid slot for them.
Don't forget to return n+1 when every slot is correct.
Don't use a hash set as your final answer — it's O(n) space and shows you missed the harder version.

approaches.name: Hash Set (O(n) space)
approaches.explanation: Easy answer. Put everything in a set, then count up from 1. Works, but fails the O(1) space requirement that's the whole point of the problem. Use as a warm-up — then derive the O(1) version.

approaches.name: Cyclic Sort (O(1) space)
approaches.explanation: The textbook in-place solution. Iterate once: for each position i, if nums[i] is in [1..n] AND the target slot doesn't already hold the right value, swap. Don't advance i — the swapped-in value might also need to move. Otherwise advance. Each element moves at most once into its final slot, so it's still O(n) amortized. The `nums[target] != nums[i]` check prevents infinite swaps on duplicates.

approaches.name: Sign Marking (O(1) space, alt)
approaches.explanation: Alternative O(1)-space solution. Uses the sign of nums[i] as a 'is i+1 present?' marker. Three passes: sanitize (kill non-positives), mark (negate the slot for each in-range value), scan (first positive slot wins). Slightly more passes than cyclic sort but each step is simpler — some prefer this for clarity.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every starting index, sum forward, check each running total. Correct but quadratic.

approaches.name: Prefix Sum + Hash Map
approaches.explanation: Maintain a running prefix sum. For each position, ask: 'have I seen a prefix sum equal to (current_prefix - k)?' If yes, that means a subarray ending here sums to k. Hash map gives O(1) lookup.

title: Valid Anagram
summary: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise. An anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of another, typically using all the original letters exactly once.
constraints: s and t consist of lowercase English letters.

examples.explanation: Same letters rearranged.
Different letters.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Two strings, check if they have the same characters in the same frequencies. Easy problem with a clear optimization path: sort → hash map → array[26]. The interview signal is whether you walk that path or stop at the first thing that works.

clarifying_questions.question: Are both strings only lowercase English letters?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, yes. This is THE optimization hint — 26 letters means you can use a fixed-size int[26] array for O(1) space instead of a hash map.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I handle Unicode or special characters?
clarifying_questions.why: Not for this problem. But interviewers often ask this as a follow-up: 'How would you extend to support emoji/Chinese?' — answer is 'switch to hash map'.

clarifying_questions.question: Is case-sensitivity required? Should I ignore whitespace?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec is clear (lowercase only), but real-world string problems often have these wrinkles. Showing you'd ask in production signals seniority.

clarifying_questions.question: If the lengths differ, can I return false immediately?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — different lengths can never be anagrams. Quick early exit avoids needless work.

clarifying_questions.question: Could the strings be empty?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints n >= 1, so no. But defensive: two empty strings would be trivial true.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: State three approaches, ordered by quality: (1) sort both, compare — O(n log n). (2) hash map of frequencies — O(n) time and space. (3) Since input is lowercase letters only, use int[26] array — O(n) time, O(1) space. Then code the best one.

edge_cases.details: "abc" vs "abcd" → false. Always check lengths first as an early exit.
Constraints forbid this. But "" and "" would technically be anagrams (true).
"aaaa" and "aaaa" → true. Confirms your frequency logic handles repeated chars correctly.
"aabb" and "abab" → true. "aabb" and "abbc" → false. Make sure your count goes both ways (+1 for s, -1 for t).

edge_cases.category: Unicode (out of scope but worth knowing)
edge_cases.details: If the problem were Unicode, array[26] wouldn't work. Switch to hash map.

common_mistakes: Using `set(s) == set(t)` — wrong! "aab" and "abb" both have set {a,b} but aren't anagrams.
Forgetting the length-check early-exit — wastes work on obviously-different inputs.
Implementing with O(n²) using .index() lookups — quadratic and unnecessary.
Using a hash map when an int[26] array would suffice (missing the optimization point).
Off-by-one in counter logic — incrementing both instead of decrementing one.

faang.introduction: Easy classic. Given two strings, return true if one is an anagram of the other.
faang.introduction_context: Easy warm-up but the interview signal is in the optimization. Strong candidates state the array[26] O(1)-space trick because the input is bounded to lowercase letters. Often paired with Group Anagrams as a follow-up.
really_testing: Recognition that 'anagram' = 'same letter frequencies'.
Layered optimization awareness: sort (O(n log n)) → hash map (O(n)) → array[26] (O(1) space).
Reading constraints carefully to spot the array[26] optimization opportunity.
Clean implementation with proper increment/decrement logic.

rubric.criteria: Asks about character set first. Lays out all three approaches (sort / hash map / array[26]). Implements array[26] for O(1) space. Tests carefully. Mentions Unicode follow-up. ~8 min.
Reaches hash map. Recognizes the array[26] optimization when prompted. Implements correctly, tests well.
Goes straight to sorting without considering O(n). Or uses `set` (wrong), needs prompting to use frequency counting.
Couldn't reach a correct O(n log n) solution. Or implemented with O(n²) using .index().

playbook: Restate the problem: 'Same letters, same frequencies'.
Ask about character set (this is THE optimization hint).
State sort approach as 'simplest correct': O(n log n).
Propose hash map: O(n) time and space.
Optimize further: 'Since lowercase letters only, I can use int[26] for O(1) space.' Score the senior points.
Add length check as early exit: if len(s) != len(t), return False.
Code the array[26]: for each char in s, ++count[c-'a']; for each char in t, --count[c-'a']. At end, all zeros.
Test on classic anagram, different-length, and 'aab' vs 'abb' (set-fooler).
Mention Unicode/emoji extension if time permits.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 7-10 min. Faster if you skip stating sort. The follow-up is usually Group Anagrams (medium) or 'first non-repeating character'.
dont_do: Don't use `set(s) == set(t)` — loses frequency information.
Don't sort when you can avoid it — slower than counting.
Don't forget the length-check early exit.
Don't use a hash map when array[26] works — signals you missed the constraint optimization.
Don't off-by-one with `c - 'a'` indexing — that's the classic C/Java bug.

approaches.name: Sort and Compare
approaches.explanation: Sort both strings; if they're anagrams, they sort to the same sequence. Simplest correct solution but suboptimal. Useful to state first if you're warming up.

approaches.name: Hash Map of Frequencies
approaches.space: O(k) where k is the size of the alphabet
approaches.explanation: Count character frequencies in each string and compare. Generalizes naturally to Unicode (replace Counter with a dict). O(n) time. Best choice when the alphabet is unbounded.

approaches.name: Array[26] (O(1) space)
approaches.explanation: Since the alphabet is bounded to 26 lowercase letters, we can use a fixed-size int[26] array. Increment for each char in s, decrement for each char in t.
