clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Phase 1 has slow=fast=head immediately after the first step. Phase 2 returns head. Worth confirming the corner case.

clarifying_questions.question: Why does the two-phase algorithm even work?
clarifying_questions.why: Showing you can sketch the proof (or admit you've memorized it) is a strong-hire signal. See playbook for the math.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: I'll do it in two phases. Phase 1: standard Floyd's to find ANY meeting point in the cycle. If fast hits None, return None. Phase 2: with slow at the meeting point, put a new pointer at head. Move both one step at a time. They'll meet at the cycle's entrance. Why: let L = distance head→entrance, C = cycle length, k = distance entrance→meet. Slow walks L+k. Fast walks 2(L+k). The difference (L+k) must be a multiple of C, so L+k = mC. Therefore L = mC - k, which is the distance from meet back around to entrance.

edge_cases.details: Phase 1's loop exits when fast hits None. Return None.
head is None; phase 1 loop never starts. Return None.
Phase 1: slow=head, fast=head, one step → slow=head, fast=head; they meet. Phase 2: ptr=head, slow=head; they're already equal. Return head.
Cycle entrance is head. The algorithm still finds it correctly.
Phase 1 takes longer; phase 2 returns the right node. O(n) overall.

common_mistakes: Returning slow at the end of phase 1 — that's just a meeting point, not the entrance.
Skipping phase 2 and returning the meet point as 'good enough' — wrong unless the cycle entrance happens to coincide.
Forgetting to handle 'no cycle' — slow == fast never happens, but the loop must terminate cleanly.
Comparing by value instead of identity — fails on lists with duplicate values.
Off-by-one when initializing fast = head.next — start both pointers at head.

faang.introduction: The 'find the cycle entrance' variant of Floyd's. Tests whether you know the two-phase trick — and bonus points if you can sketch the proof.
faang.introduction_context: Direct follow-up to Linked List Cycle. Onsite question at Google, Meta, Microsoft. Failing to articulate phase 2 is the most common stumble.
really_testing: Whether you know phase 2 exists (most people only remember phase 1).
Math intuition: L = mC - k.
Clean, bug-free Floyd's code with proper null checks.
Edge cases: empty list, self-loop on head, no cycle.

rubric.criteria: Names the two-phase algorithm. Sketches the L+k = mC math. Codes both phases cleanly in 10 min.
Recalls phase 2 exists; codes it correctly. Doesn't derive the math but applies it.
Stuck after detecting the cycle. Returns the meeting point as the answer.
Cannot complete Floyd's detection. Or solves with O(n) hash and doesn't recognize the O(1) is reachable.

playbook: Restate: 'return the node where the cycle begins (or None)'.
Pitch the two-phase: 'phase 1 = Floyd's to find a meeting point; phase 2 = walk one pointer from head and one from meet at speed 1; they meet at the entrance'.
Phase 1 code: slow = fast = head. while fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; if slow is fast: break. else: return None.
Phase 2 code: ptr = head. while ptr is not slow: ptr = ptr.next; slow = slow.next. return ptr.

Math: L = head→entrance, C = cycle len, k = entrance→meet. Slow steps = L+k. Fast = 2(L+k). Fast laps slow some m times: L+k = mC. So L = mC-k, meaning from head and from meet, walking L steps each, both land on entrance.
Walk on [3,2,0,-4] pos=1: phase 1 meet inside cycle. Phase 2 from head=3 and from meet, both meet at node value 2.
Edge: no cycle — phase 1 exits via the while condition; return None.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Follow-up: Find the Duplicate Number (LC 287) — same algorithm on an array, mind-blow moment for the interviewer if you spot it.
dont_do: Don't skip phase 2 — meeting point != entrance.
Don't return slow.val before phase 2 — wrong on most cases.
Don't forget the 'no cycle' early return.
Don't compare nodes by value.
Don't try to count the cycle length and use that — works but uses extra steps and obscures the elegance.

approaches.name: Two-Phase Floyd's
approaches.explanation: Phase 1 detects a cycle (Floyd's tortoise-and-hare) and lands slow somewhere inside the cycle. Phase 2 exploits the geometry: walking head→entrance is the same number of steps as walking from the meeting point back around to the entrance. So putting a fresh pointer at head and advancing both at speed 1 makes them collide at the entrance. O(n) time, O(1) space.

approaches.name: Hash Set (warm-up)
approaches.explanation: Walk the list, recording each node. The first node already in the set is the cycle entry. Correct but uses O(n) space. Useful as a warm-up; the interviewer is looking for the O(1) version.

title: Middle of the Linked List
summary: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node. If there are two middle nodes (even length), return the second one. (Here we return the middle node's value to make it testable.)
constraints: Number of nodes in the list is in [1, 100].

examples.explanation: Five nodes; the middle is index 2 (value 3).
Six nodes; two middles (3 and 4). Return the second, value 4.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Fast/slow pointers. Slow moves 1, fast moves 2. When fast reaches the end (or runs off it), slow is at the middle. For even length, slow naturally lands on the SECOND middle — which is what the problem asks for.

clarifying_questions.question: If even length, do I return the first or second middle?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says the second. The naive fast/slow loop naturally returns the second middle, so we just confirm rather than tweak the algorithm.

clarifying_questions.question: One pass or two passes allowed?
clarifying_questions.why: Two-pass (count length, then walk to the middle) works and is O(n). Single-pass with fast/slow is more elegant and what the interviewer is looking for.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the list be empty?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, length >= 1. Worth a sentence to confirm.

clarifying_questions.question: Do I need to handle modifications?
clarifying_questions.why: No — read-only. Algorithm is non-destructive.

clarifying_questions.question: What should I return — node or value?
clarifying_questions.why: LC asks for the node. Confirm whether the interviewer wants the node or .val. In our test wrapper we return .val.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two pointers from head. Slow += 1, fast += 2. Stop when fast.next is None (odd length, fast at last) or fast is None (even length, fast walked off). Both cases leave slow at the middle. For [1,2,3,4,5]: slow walks 1→2→3, fast walks 1→3→5 (then stops). Slow = 3. For [1,2,3,4,5,6]: slow walks 1→2→3→4, fast walks 1→3→5→None. Slow = 4 (the second middle). Done.

edge_cases.details: Slow = fast = head. fast.next is None → loop doesn't execute. Return head's value.
After one step: slow at 2, fast at None. Loop exits. Return value of slow = second node. ✓
Standard middle. Easy.
Spec wants the second middle, fast/slow naturally produces it. Worth verifying once mentally.
Just O(n). No special handling — same code path.

common_mistakes: Returning the FIRST middle on even length — usually a side effect of initializing slow=head, fast=head.next.
Wrong loop condition: `while fast and fast.next` is correct; `while fast.next and fast.next.next` returns the first middle on even.
Counting length first and walking n//2 — works but two passes; less elegant.
Forgetting that one-node lists are valid — must return without entering the loop.
Trying to use Floyd's cycle-style 'meet' logic — wrong pattern, no cycle here.

faang.introduction: An 'easy' question testing the fast/slow pointer pattern. Asked as a warm-up before the harder cycle problems.
faang.introduction_context: Phone screen filler at Amazon, Microsoft, Bloomberg. Often paired with 'now use this idea to detect a cycle' as a setup question.
really_testing: Whether you reach for fast/slow rather than count-then-walk.
Loop-condition discipline for the second-middle case.
Comfort with single-pass linked-list traversal.
Confirming the second-middle rule before coding.

rubric.criteria: Codes fast/slow in 2-4 min. Asks 'first or second middle?' before coding. Verifies on an even-length example.
Two-pass solution, then refactors to fast/slow when prompted. Correct final answer.
Returns first middle on even (off-by-one). Or buggy fast/slow with NoneType crashes.
Can't articulate any single-pass approach. Or builds a Python list of all nodes then indexes.

playbook: Restate: 'middle node, second middle if even length'.
Confirm: 'second middle on even — right?' Yes.
Pitch fast/slow: 'slow steps 1, fast steps 2 — when fast walks off, slow is at the middle'.
Code: slow = fast = head. while fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next. return slow.
Walk odd: [1,2,3,4,5] → slow ends at 3. Walk even: [1,2,3,4,5,6] → slow ends at 4. ✅
Edge: [1] → loop doesn't run; return head. [1,2] → slow advances once; return second.
Done in 3 min — interviewer probably moves on to cycle detection.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 3-5 min. Direct follow-up: Linked List Cycle (extend slow/fast to detect a cycle).
dont_do: Don't count length then walk — works but two passes.
Don't initialize fast = head.next — produces first middle on even.
Don't convert to a list and index — defeats the linked-list practice.
Don't use recursion — overkill for O(1) space.
Don't forget the single-node edge case (loop doesn't execute).

approaches.name: Fast and Slow Pointers (one pass)
approaches.explanation: Classic fast/slow. Slow moves 1, fast moves 2. When fast reaches None (even length) or fast.next is None (odd length), slow sits at the middle. For even lengths, slow naturally lands on the SECOND middle — exactly what the spec wants. Beautiful one-loop solution.

approaches.name: Two Pass (count then walk)
approaches.explanation: Walk the list once to count nodes, then walk again to position target = n // 2 (0-indexed). Correct but two passes. Show this only if you mention 'I could do this in one pass with fast/slow' — that's the version they want.

title: Happy Number
summary: A number is 'happy' if repeatedly replacing it with the sum of the squares of its digits eventually reaches 1. Otherwise the sequence loops endlessly. Given n, return True if n is happy.
starter_code:
def isHappy(n):
    # Return True if repeatedly summing squared digits leads to 1.
    # Otherwise (sequence cycles), return False.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: The sequence either reaches 1 (happy) or enters a cycle. So it's literally a cycle-detection problem on an integer sequence. Two equally valid approaches: a hash set (track every value seen) or Floyd's fast/slow on the sequence. Both are O(log n) per step (digit count) and the sequence has bounded length.

clarifying_questions.question: What counts as 'happy' exactly — must reach 1, or just stop?
clarifying_questions.why: Must reach 1. Anything else means a cycle. Pinning this down avoids off-by-one panic on n=1.

clarifying_questions.question: Is n bounded?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes, up to 2^31 - 1. The sequence stays manageable — values quickly drop to small ranges because squaring digits collapses big numbers fast.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I use a hash set or Floyd's?
clarifying_questions.why: Either is fine. Floyd's gets a brownie point for O(1) extra space. Hash set is easier to write — use that as a safe first answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Are negatives or zero possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints n >= 1. So no. Worth confirming so you don't waste time handling them.

clarifying_questions.question: Why is the sequence guaranteed to cycle if not happy?
clarifying_questions.why: Because squared-digit-sum of a k-digit number is at most 81*k, which is much smaller than the number itself for k > 3. The sequence is bounded → must cycle by pigeonhole.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two interpretations. (1) Cycle on a stream of integers — Floyd's applies. Slow = squared_sum(slow), fast = squared_sum(squared_sum(fast)). They meet either at 1 (happy) or somewhere in a cycle (not happy). (2) Hash set: keep applying squared_sum, return True if you hit 1, False if you hit a value already in the set. Both work; pick the one you're confident writing.

edge_cases.details: Already 1. Return True immediately (or the algorithm handles it on iteration 0).
2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9 are all unhappy. The classic cycle is 4 → 16 → 37 → 58 → 89 → 145 → 42 → 20 → 4.
Even 10^9 collapses within a few steps — max squared-digit-sum is 9² * 10 = 810. Sequence quickly enters the [1, 800] range.
100 → 1 → done. The 0 contributes nothing to the squared sum.
1 and 7 are happy; 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9 are not. Worth memorizing if you want to sound impressive.

common_mistakes: Infinite loop: forgetting the cycle-detection mechanism (no set, no Floyd's) — code never returns.
Treating the digit-sum as just sum-of-digits (not squared) — different problem.
Confusing 'happy' with 'reaches a single digit' — must specifically reach 1.
Buggy digit extraction — use `while n: digit = n % 10; total += digit*digit; n //= 10`.
Returning early on n == 0 — out of spec; n >= 1 guaranteed, but 0 would loop forever anyway.

faang.introduction: Cycle detection on a number sequence. Tests whether you can map an abstract problem onto the fast/slow or hash-set pattern.
faang.introduction_context: Asked at Amazon, Bloomberg, sometimes as a 'soft' phone screen — easy to set up, has multiple acceptable solutions, lets the interviewer probe complexity tradeoffs.
really_testing: Recognizing 'either reaches 1 or cycles' as cycle-detection.
Clean digit extraction.
Choosing between hash set (O(n) space, easy) and Floyd's (O(1) space, slightly trickier).
Why the sequence is bounded (so the cycle MUST happen).

rubric.criteria: Reframes as cycle detection in seconds. Codes Floyd's cleanly. Or codes hash set then mentions O(1) Floyd's variant. Argues bounded-sequence.
Solves with hash set. Doesn't reach Floyd's without prompting. Correct.
Solves but can't articulate why termination is guaranteed. Or buggy digit extraction.
Code loops forever on unhappy numbers. Or fails to compute squared-digit-sum correctly.

playbook: Restate: 'apply squared-digit-sum repeatedly; True if reaches 1, False if loops forever'.
Insight: 'this either reaches 1 or cycles — it's cycle detection on a stream'.
Helper: def sq(n): total = 0; while n: total += (n % 10) ** 2; n //= 10. return total.
Hash-set version: seen = set(); while n != 1 and n not in seen: seen.add(n); n = sq(n). return n == 1.
Floyd's version: slow = n; fast = sq(n). while fast != 1 and slow != fast: slow = sq(slow); fast = sq(sq(fast)). return fast == 1.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-10 min. Follow-up: 'why is termination guaranteed?' Answer: squared-digit-sum of k-digit number ≤ 81k, so sequence is bounded; pigeonhole gives a cycle.
dont_do: Don't write code without a termination mechanism — infinite loop on unhappy inputs.
Don't sum digits without squaring — different problem.
Don't use string conversion if you can avoid it — `str(n)` works but `n % 10` and `n //= 10` is faster and shows comfort with arithmetic.
Don't claim the sequence is unbounded — it's not, and that's why this problem is finite.
Don't forget to handle n=1 (trivially happy).

approaches.name: Hash Set
approaches.time: O(log n) per step, bounded total steps
approaches.explanation: Apply squared-digit-sum repeatedly. Track every value in a set. If we hit 1, happy. If we re-encounter a value, cycle → not happy. The sequence is bounded by the math (large numbers collapse fast), so this terminates. Clean and easy to get right under pressure.

approaches.name: Floyd's on the Sequence
approaches.time: O(log n) per step, bounded total steps
approaches.explanation: Same idea as Linked List Cycle, but applied to a stream of integers. Slow advances one step (one application of sq); fast advances two. If the stream reaches 1, fast hits 1 first. If it cycles, slow and fast eventually meet at some value ≠ 1. O(1) extra space — the textbook trick to drop the hash set.

title: Subsets
summary: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets. Return the solution in any order.
constraints: All the numbers of nums are unique.

examples.explanation: All 2^3 = 8 subsets of {1,2,3}.
Empty set and the single-element set.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Power set. For each element, the choice is binary: include it or skip it. That gives 2^n total subsets. Three classic ways to generate them: recursive backtracking (the textbook answer), iterative 'build by extending existing subsets', or bit-manipulation (each int 0..2^n-1 is a subset mask). Backtracking is the most generalizable and the typical FAANG expected answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Are duplicate elements allowed in input?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says unique. If duplicates were allowed, you'd need extra dedup logic (Subsets II variant). Knowing the variant matters.

clarifying_questions.question: Output order — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says any order. Saves the cost of sorting. Different algorithms naturally produce different orders.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty subset — should I include it?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — the empty set is always part of the power set. Standard contract but worth confirming.

clarifying_questions.question: How big is n?
clarifying_questions.why: Constraint: n ≤ 10. So 2^n ≤ 1024 — small. Even O(2^n × n) is fast. No need for sub-exponential. Just be correct.

clarifying_questions.question: Are mutated subsets shared between iterations?
clarifying_questions.why: If you append the same list to the result repeatedly, all entries point to the same object. Make sure to append a COPY (result.append(current[:])). Classic bug.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking framing: at each index, two choices — include nums[i] in the current subset, or skip it. Recurse. When index reaches n, record the current subset (as a COPY). The recursion tree has 2^n leaves, each a distinct subset.

edge_cases.details: [] → [[]]. Just the empty subset itself. Recursion terminates immediately.
[5] → [[], [5]]. Two subsets — empty and full.
If you forget to copy `current` when appending, all 2^n results will be the same final list. Test by checking len(set(map(tuple, result))) == 2^n.
2^10 = 1024 subsets. Should run in milliseconds. If your code times out, you have an algorithmic bug.

common_mistakes: Appending `current` directly to result (shared reference) instead of `current[:]` (copy). Classic Python bug.
Forgetting to pop after recursing — leaves stale elements in `current` for subsequent calls.
Off-by-one on the recursion index — starting at 0 vs i+1 changes the semantics.
Trying to use sets — but subsets in Python are lists, and you'd need tuples for hashability.
Generating duplicates because you don't track which element-positions you've already considered.

faang.introduction: Generate all subsets of an array. Classic backtracking — the gateway problem to the entire backtracking pattern.
faang.introduction_context: Tests the choose-or-don't recursive pattern. Sets up follow-ups in Subsets II (with duplicates), Combinations (size-k subsets), Permutations.
really_testing: Recursive backtracking pattern recognition.
Correct state management: include → recurse → undo (the backtrack step).
Awareness of the shared-reference / copy-before-append bug.
Comfort with O(2^n) being acceptable when n is small.

rubric.criteria: Proposes backtracking immediately. Articulates 'include or skip' decision. Correctly copies the current list when appending. Walks through tree mentally for [1,2,3]. ~8 min.
Reaches backtracking. Initial version has the shared-reference bug; catches it after testing. Correctness in 12 min.
Uses iterative approach without explanation, or backtracking that produces duplicates. Off-by-one on the index.
Can't structure the recursion. Doesn't recognize the choose-or-skip pattern.

playbook: Restate: 'Generate all 2^n subsets of nums.'
Confirm any-order output and that input is duplicate-free.
Propose backtracking: 'At index i, choose to include nums[i] or skip. Recurse. When i == n, record current subset.'
Code: def backtrack(start, current): result.append(current[:]); for i in range(start, n): current.append(nums[i]); backtrack(i+1, current); current.pop().
Note the critical `current[:]` — append a COPY, not a reference.
Test on [1] → should produce [[], [1]].
Mention iterative 'add to existing' alternative and bit-manipulation, but say backtracking is the most generalizable.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-ups: Subsets II (with duplicates, need to sort + skip), Combinations (only size-k), Permutations (order matters).
dont_do: Don't append current directly — append current[:] (a copy).
Don't forget the pop after recursion — leaves stale state for siblings.
Don't use a set to dedupe — input is guaranteed unique. Adding a set check signals you didn't read the spec.
Don't use bit manipulation as your primary answer if asked about backtracking — show the right tool for the right interview signal.
Don't fail to record the empty subset at the START of backtracking (record at every node, not just leaves).

approaches.name: Iterative — Add to Existing
approaches.explanation: Start with the empty subset. For each new num, take every existing subset and create a new subset with num appended. Doubles the result set each iteration. O(n) iterations × O(2^n) work per iteration = O(n × 2^n) total.

approaches.name: Backtracking
approaches.code:
def subsets(nums):
    result = []
    current = []
    def backtrack(start):
        result.append(current[:])  # COPY — critical
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            current.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1)
            current.pop()
    backtrack(0)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Recursive choose-or-skip. At each call, record the current subset (as a COPY), then for each remaining element, include it and recurse. The pop() at the end is the 'backtrack' — undoes the choice so the next iteration can try a different one. Visits each of 2^n subsets exactly once.

approaches.name: Bit Manipulation
approaches.code:
def subsets(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    result = []
    for mask in range(1 << n):
        subset = [nums[i] for i in range(n) if mask & (1 << i)]
        result.append(subset)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Each integer mask from 0 to 2^n - 1 represents a subset: bit i is 1 iff nums[i] is included. Iterate through all 2^n masks, build each subset by checking bits. Clean and compact but less generalizable than backtracking (e.g., harder to add early-termination).

title: Permutations
summary: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all the possible permutations. You can return the answer in any order.
constraints: All the integers of nums are unique.

examples.explanation: All 3! = 6 orderings of [1,2,3].
2! = 2 orderings.
Single element — one permutation.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Generate all n! orderings of nums. Classic backtracking. At each step, pick any unused element, add to current permutation, recurse, then unpick. Track 'used' via a set or by swapping in place.

clarifying_questions.question: Are elements distinct or could there be duplicates?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says distinct. If duplicates existed, you'd need to skip duplicate branches (Permutations II variant). Important distinction.

clarifying_questions.question: Output order — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says any order. Saves work.

clarifying_questions.question: How large is n?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 6. n! = 720 max — very small. No optimization needed beyond correctness.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I generate permutations in lexicographic order?
clarifying_questions.why: Not required. But there's a classic 'Next Permutation' problem if lex order matters.

clarifying_questions.question: In-place mutation vs build-new-list?
clarifying_questions.why: Both work. Swapping in place is O(1) per step; building a new list is O(n) per step. Either is fine for n=6.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking: at each level of recursion, try every available number. Use a 'used' set or boolean array to track which are in the current permutation. When the current permutation has length n, record it. Then backtrack — pop the last element and try the next option.

edge_cases.details: [1] → [[1]]. Single permutation.
Spec says n ≥ 1, but defensive: [] → [[]] (the empty permutation).
[-1,-2,-3] — algorithm is value-agnostic, works fine.

common_mistakes: Appending `current` directly instead of `current[:]` (shared reference bug — all results become the same final permutation).
Forgetting to mark used/unused around the recursive call.
Off-by-one on the recursion depth (n vs n-1).
Trying to dedupe when input has unique elements — wasted check.
Confusing permutations with combinations or subsets (different problems).

faang.introduction: Backtracking essentials. Generate all permutations of a distinct-integer array.
faang.introduction_context: Often paired with Subsets and Combinations as the 'backtracking trio'. Tests the same pattern (choose-recurse-unchoose) with the twist that order matters.
really_testing: Backtracking template recall (choose-recurse-unchoose).
Correct state management — track which elements are used.
Awareness of the COPY bug (append current[:], not current).
Understanding that permutations differ from subsets/combinations (order matters).

rubric.criteria: Proposes backtracking immediately. Clean code with 'used' tracking. Correctly copies current. Walks through tree mentally. ~8 min.
Reaches backtracking. Has the shared-reference bug initially; catches it after testing.
Tries iterative without justification. Or backtracking with off-by-one. Or generates duplicates.
Can't structure the recursion. Confuses permutations with subsets.

playbook: Restate: 'Generate all n! orderings.'
Confirm distinct elements, any order output.
Propose backtracking with 'used' tracking.
Code: result=[]; current=[]; used=[False]*n.
Recurse: if len(current) == n, append current[:]. Else for each i: if not used[i]: used[i]=True; current.append(nums[i]); recurse; current.pop(); used[i]=False.
Optionally show the in-place swap variant.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Common follow-ups: Permutations II (with duplicates — need to sort + skip), Next Permutation (single-step in lex order).
dont_do: Don't append current directly — copy via current[:].
Don't forget to unmark used after recursion (the 'unchoose' step).
Don't dedupe when input is unique — wasted code.
Don't generate subsets instead of permutations — they're different.
Don't use itertools.permutations() as your answer — shows library knowledge but not algorithm understanding.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Used Array
approaches.code:
def permute(nums):
    result = []
    current = []
    used = [False] * len(nums)
    def backtrack():
        if len(current) == len(nums):
            result.append(current[:])
            return
        for i in range(len(nums)):
            if used[i]:
                continue
            used[i] = True
            current.append(nums[i])
            backtrack()
            current.pop()
            used[i] = False
    backtrack()
    return result
approaches.explanation: Classic backtracking. Maintain a 'used' array. At each recursion level, try every unused element. Mark used → recurse → unmark. When current is full, record a COPY (current[:]). Each of n! permutations is built once.

approaches.name: Backtracking with In-Place Swap
approaches.explanation: Instead of using an extra 'used' array, swap elements in place. At depth d, fix position d by swapping in each remaining element. Recurse for position d+1. Swap back to undo. More memory-efficient but slightly harder to read.

title: Combinations
summary: Given two integers n and k, return all possible combinations of k numbers chosen from the range [1, n]. You may return the answer in any order.

examples.explanation: All C(4,2)=6 size-2 combinations of {1,2,3,4}.
Only one combination: pick the single element.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Subsets-of-size-k variant. Backtracking: at each step, pick the next number from the remaining range. Track the start index to avoid duplicate combinations (since order doesn't matter, only pick increasing sequences).

clarifying_questions.question: Order within combinations — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Combinations are unordered ([1,2] == [2,1]). To avoid duplicates, generate in increasing order. Confirms the start-index pattern.

clarifying_questions.question: Output order — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says any order. Saves work.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the same number appear twice in a combination?
clarifying_questions.why: No — combinations are choices WITHOUT replacement. {1,1,2} isn't valid.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the upper bound on n?
clarifying_questions.why: n ≤ 20, k ≤ n. C(20,10) = 184756 — feasible. Worth confirming for time budget.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the empty combination (k=0) possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, k ≥ 1. So no. But defensive: k=0 should return [[]].

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking, like Subsets but constrained to exact size k. Track a start index (the smallest number we can still pick). At each step, pick a number from [start, n], add to current, recurse with start+1. When current has length k, record it. Pruning: if current has c numbers and you need k-c more, no point continuing if there aren't k-c numbers left in [start, n].

edge_cases.details: n=3, k=1 → [[1], [2], [3]]. Singleton combinations.
n=3, k=3 → [[1,2,3]]. Only one combination — the full set.
[[]] — just the empty combination. Defensive code can handle this.
n=20, k=10 → 184756 combinations. Pruning helps but exponential growth is fundamental.
With pruning, recursion stops when remaining numbers < remaining slots. Significant speedup at the limits.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to copy current[:] when appending (shared-reference bug).
Not tracking start index — produces duplicates like [1,2] and [2,1].
Off-by-one on the range (n vs n+1 — must include n).
Forgetting the pop() after recursion — leaves stale state.
Not pruning when remaining numbers < remaining slots — works correctly but slower.

faang.introduction: Backtracking variant. Generate all size-k combinations of integers from 1 to n.
faang.introduction_context: Cousin of Subsets. Tests the 'start index' technique to avoid duplicates in combinatorial generation.
really_testing: Backtracking with constraints (target size k).
Using start index to avoid duplicate combinations.
Optional pruning when remaining < required.
Distinguishing combinations from permutations.

rubric.criteria: Recognizes as Subsets-of-size-k. Uses start index correctly. Adds pruning. Codes cleanly. ~8 min.
Backtracking works without pruning. Correct output.
Produces duplicates (missing start index). Or buggy recursion.
Can't structure the recursion. Confuses with permutations.

playbook: Restate: 'all size-k combinations from [1, n]'.
Confirm: combinations (unordered), no replacement.
Propose backtracking with start index (only increasing sequences).
Code: result=[]; current=[]; def backtrack(start): if len(current)==k: result.append(current[:]); return; for i in range(start, n+1): current.append(i); backtrack(i+1); current.pop(). backtrack(1).
Optional: add pruning — if (k - len(current)) > (n - start + 1), no point continuing.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 7-10 min. Follow-up: Combination Sum (with repetition).
dont_do: Don't generate permutations of size k — those are larger and duplicate.
Don't forget the start index — produces duplicates.
Don't forget current[:] copy.
Don't forget the +1 on range(start, n+1) — must include n.
Don't fail to pop after recursion.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Start Index
approaches.explanation: Standard backtracking. The `start` parameter ensures we only pick increasing sequences, avoiding duplicates like [1,2] and [2,1]. When current reaches size k, record a copy. Recursion depth is k; total work is O(C(n,k) × k).

approaches.name: Backtracking with Pruning
approaches.time: O(C(n,k) × k) but faster in practice
approaches.code:
def combine(n, k):
    result = []
    current = []
    def backtrack(start):
        if len(current) == k:
            result.append(current[:])
            return
        # Pruning: if remaining slots > remaining numbers, no valid combinations
        needed = k - len(current)
        for i in range(start, n - needed + 2):
            current.append(i)
            backtrack(i + 1)
            current.pop()
    backtrack(1)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Same algorithm with one pruning trick: at each level, we only need to consider numbers up to n - needed + 1 (where 'needed' is how many more we must pick). Picking a larger start would leave insufficient numbers to complete the combination. Saves significant work near the leaves.

title: N-Queens
summary: Place n queens on an n×n chessboard so that no two queens attack each other. Return all distinct solutions; each solution is a list of strings where 'Q' is a queen and '.' is an empty square.

examples.explanation: Two distinct 4-queens arrangements. One queen per row, one per column, no shared diagonal.
A 1×1 board: place the single queen. Trivially valid.

starter_code:
def solveNQueens(n):
    # Return a list of all valid n-queens board configurations.
    # Each configuration is a list of n strings of length n.
    pass

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Classic backtracking. Place one queen per row — that automatically handles 'no two queens share a row'. For each row, try every column; reject if the chosen column conflicts with any previously placed queen on column, '↖ diagonal' (row - col), or '↗ diagonal' (row + col). Use sets for those three to make conflict checks O(1).

clarifying_questions.question: Return all solutions, or just the count?
clarifying_questions.why: LC 51 asks for all boards (list of lists of strings). LC 52 (N-Queens II) just asks for the count. Different output shape — confirm which one.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the bound on n?
clarifying_questions.why: n ≤ 9. Search space is manageable. Above n=15 even good backtracking gets slow. Bound tells you the answer is allowed to be exponential.

clarifying_questions.question: Output format — list of strings per board?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes: each board is a list of n strings of length n, with 'Q' for queen and '.' otherwise. Easy to mess up by returning column indices only.

clarifying_questions.question: How should distinct solutions be defined — should rotations/reflections count separately?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — LC counts them separately. n=4 has 2 boards; symmetry would reduce to 1. Worth confirming.

clarifying_questions.question: Does an empty board count for n=0?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints n ≥ 1, so moot. But defensive thinking: [[]] for n=0 — one trivial solution.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: One queen per row means we never need to check rows. So I'll iterate rows top-to-bottom, picking a column for each. For each candidate column, three sets reject it instantly: columns_used (col), neg_diagonal (row - col), pos_diagonal (row + col). On row N we've placed N queens — record the board and backtrack. Build the board lazily from the column assignments at record-time, or maintain a board list and place/unplace 'Q' as you go.

edge_cases.details: Single cell, one trivial solution: [['Q']]. Important to not return empty.
Provably no solutions. Function must return [] without erroring.
92 and 352 solutions respectively. Make sure recursion handles this without exploding — the diagonals pruning is what keeps it tractable.
Rebuild from queen positions at record-time — don't append the in-progress board reference, you'd capture mutations. Or copy with [:].
After recursion returns, remove the queen from all three sets and (if maintaining a board) restore '.'. Forgetting any one of these leaks state into sibling branches.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to remove from cols/diag1/diag2 after backtracking — sibling branches inherit stale state.
Confusing diag1 and diag2 — '↖' is row - col (constant on that diagonal), '↗' is row + col.
Appending the live board reference instead of a copy — final result has duplicates of the last state.
Checking conflicts by scanning previously placed queens (O(n) per check) instead of using sets (O(1)).
Returning column indices instead of the requested 'Q'/'.' string format.

faang.introduction: The textbook hard backtracking problem. Mostly tests whether you can structure a recursive search cleanly, use sets to make conflict checks O(1), and remember to undo state on the way back up.
faang.introduction_context: Frequent in onsites at Google and Meta when interviewers want to see disciplined recursion. Solving it cleanly in ~20 min with sets and a well-defined recursive structure is what they're looking for.
really_testing: Clean recursive structure (one row at a time avoids redundant work).
Three-set conflict tracking (cols, '↖' diagonal as row-col, '↗' as row+col).
State undo on backtrack — the #1 bug in this problem family.
Correctly building the output format (strings, not indices).

rubric.criteria: Proposes 'one queen per row, three sets, backtrack' within minutes. Codes it correctly with undo. Walks n=4 by hand. 15-20 min.
Gets a working solution with O(n)-per-check conflict scanning. Correct but slower. Or needs a hint on the diagonals formulas.
Buggy backtrack — forgets to undo, or wrong diagonal index. Misses n=2/3 = [].
Cannot structure a recursive search. Or tries to generate all permutations (n!) and filter.

playbook: Restate: 'place n queens, no two attack, return all boards in string form'.
Clarifying: all boards or count? Output format strings?
Insight: 'one queen per row — we just need to pick a column for each row'.
Conflict sets: cols, neg_diag = row - col, pos_diag = row + col. Each O(1).

Code: def backtrack(row, queens, cols, d1, d2): if row == n: result.append(build(queens)); return. for col in range(n): if col in cols or row-col in d1 or row+col in d2: continue. add to all three, queens.append(col), recurse, then pop and remove.
Test on n=4: trace by hand or run; expect 2 boards.
Edge: n=2 and n=3 — verify the search returns [] (no placements survive conflicts).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 15-20 min. Follow-up: N-Queens II (count only — same recursion, just increment a counter). Sudoku Solver (same pattern, more constraints).
dont_do: Don't scan all previously placed queens for each check — use three sets.
Don't forget to remove from sets when backtracking.
Don't append the live board list — copy with [:] or build from queens at record time.
Don't generate all n! column permutations and filter — misses the point of backtracking.
Don't try to handle one-queen-per-column AND one-queen-per-row constraints separately — iterating row by row collapses one of them automatically.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Conflict Sets
approaches.code:
def solveNQueens(n):
    result = []
    queens = []          # queens[row] = column
    cols = set()
    neg_diag = set()     # row - col
    pos_diag = set()     # row + col
    
    def build():
        return ['.' * c + 'Q' + '.' * (n - c - 1) for c in queens]
    
    def backtrack(row):
        if row == n:
            result.append(build())
            return
        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row - col) in neg_diag or (row + col) in pos_diag:
                continue
            queens.append(col)
            cols.add(col)
            neg_diag.add(row - col)
            pos_diag.add(row + col)
            
            backtrack(row + 1)
            
            queens.pop()
            cols.remove(col)
            neg_diag.remove(row - col)
            pos_diag.remove(row + col)
    
    backtrack(0)
    return result
approaches.explanation: The canonical solution. We place exactly one queen per row, so the recursion depth is n. Three sets track conflicts in O(1): cols for column conflicts, neg_diag (row-col is constant on a '↖' diagonal), pos_diag (row+col is constant on a '↗' diagonal). When row == n we've placed all queens; reconstruct the board from queens and append a copy. After each recursive call, remove the queen from all three sets to restore state for sibling branches. Pruning makes this dramatically faster than n!.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Boolean Arrays (no sets)
approaches.code:
def solveNQueens(n):
    result = []
    queens = []
    cols = [False] * n
    neg_diag = [False] * (2 * n - 1)   # index row - col + (n - 1)
    pos_diag = [False] * (2 * n - 1)   # index row + col
    
    def backtrack(row):
        if row == n:
            result.append(['.' * c + 'Q' + '.' * (n - c - 1) for c in queens])
            return
        for col in range(n):
            d1 = row - col + n - 1
            d2 = row + col
            if cols[col] or neg_diag[d1] or pos_diag[d2]:
                continue
            queens.append(col)
            cols[col] = neg_diag[d1] = pos_diag[d2] = True
            backtrack(row + 1)
            queens.pop()
            cols[col] = neg_diag[d1] = pos_diag[d2] = False
    
    backtrack(0)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Same algorithm, but boolean arrays instead of sets. The indexing for neg_diag shifts by (n-1) to avoid negative indices. Same asymptotic complexity; tiny constant-factor speedup on large boards. Mostly worth knowing if you want to write it in a language without efficient hash sets, or if the interviewer asks for an arrays-only version.

title: Word Search
summary: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically neighboring). The same letter cell may not be used more than once.
constraints: board and word consist of only lowercase and uppercase English letters.

examples.explanation: B(0,1) can only be used once. After A(0,0)→B(0,1)→C(0,2), there's no way back to a different B.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: DFS from every starting cell. For each path, mark visited cells (temporarily replace the char or use a separate set). When the path matches the word, return True. When it fails, backtrack (restore the cell). The 'same letter cell may not be used twice' constraint is what forces backtracking — without it, a simple existence-of-each-letter check would suffice.

clarifying_questions.question: Can cells be reused?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec explicitly says NO — same cell can't appear twice in a path. This is what makes backtracking necessary.

clarifying_questions.question: What does 'adjacent' mean — 4 directions or 8?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says horizontally or vertically — 4 directions (up/down/left/right). Diagonal is excluded.

clarifying_questions.question: Case sensitive?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says both cases, treat as distinct. 'A' and 'a' are different.

clarifying_questions.question: Word length vs grid size?
clarifying_questions.why: Word up to 15, grid up to 6×6=36 cells. If word > 36, return false immediately. Worth confirming for early exit.

clarifying_questions.question: Is mutating the board OK?
clarifying_questions.why: If yes, you can use the board itself as the visited tracker (temporarily replace cell with '#' during recursion). Saves O(m×n) space. Most interviewers expect this for senior signal.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking from each cell. For each (i,j), try to match word[0] there, then recursively try to match word[1] in adjacent cells, etc. Mark visited cells to prevent reuse. Restore on backtrack. Pruning: skip if board[i][j] != word[k] immediately. Worst case O(m × n × 4^L) where L is word length, but typically much less due to pruning.

edge_cases.details: board=[['A']], word='A' → true. Match at start, word fully consumed.
board=[['A']], word='B' → false.
If word.length > m*n, can't possibly fit (can't reuse cells). Early exit.
Must search ALL 4 directions, not just forward.
Like 'XYZ' on a grid with only 'A's. DFS prunes quickly via the first-char check.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to mark cell as visited before recursing → infinite loop or wrong answer on cycles.
Forgetting to UNMARK cell after backtracking → cells stay 'used' across different paths from different starts.
Using a separate visited set when modifying the board would work — costs extra O(m×n) space.
Off-by-one on the word index (k vs k+1).
Not pruning early when board[i][j] != word[k] — runs the recursion needlessly.

faang.introduction: Word Search. Given a grid and a word, can the word be formed by following adjacent cells (no reuse)?
faang.introduction_context: Classic 2D backtracking. Common at FAANG. Tests DFS on a grid + state restoration. Often a follow-up: 'Word Search II' generalizes to many words with a Trie.
really_testing: DFS on a 2D grid (handle bounds, 4 directions).
Backtracking discipline (mark, recurse, unmark).
In-place visited tracking (the senior optimization).
Pruning at the first character mismatch for performance.

rubric.criteria: Articulates DFS + backtracking. Uses in-place marking (modify board). Prunes on char mismatch. Clean code. ~12 min.
Reaches the algorithm with an external visited set. Code correct but uses extra space.
Forgets to unmark cells on backtrack. Or doesn't try all 4 directions. Or buggy bounds.
Can't structure the recursion. Can't handle grid traversal correctly.

playbook: Restate: 'Find word in grid by following adjacent cells, no reuse.'
Confirm: 4-direction adjacency, no cell reuse, case sensitive.
Propose backtracking DFS from each starting cell.
Sketch: for each (i,j), call dfs(i,j,0). dfs(i,j,k) returns True if word[k:] starts at (i,j).
Inside dfs: bounds check, mismatch check, mark, recurse to 4 neighbors with k+1, unmark, return any-success.
Show in-place marking: temporarily set board[i][j] = '#' (or any non-letter char) during recursion. Restore on backtrack.
Test on the example. Trace 'ABCCED' to confirm the path.
Discuss complexity: O(m×n × 4^L) worst case, typically way less due to pruning.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Follow-up: Word Search II (multiple words via Trie).
dont_do: Don't forget to unmark cells on backtrack — leaves stale state.
Don't search only 1-2 directions — must try all 4.
Don't use an extra m×n visited matrix when in-place marking works.
Don't return early on first cell mismatch without trying the recursion — but DO prune the first char.
Don't try to be clever with global state — keep recursion pure.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Extra Visited Set
approaches.space: O(m × n) for visited + O(L) recursion
approaches.explanation: DFS from each cell. Use a 'visited' set to prevent reuse. Mark on entry, unmark on backtrack. Try all 4 directions. The 4^L term is the recursion fanout; m × n is the number of starting cells. Pruning (the char mismatch check) significantly reduces actual runtime.

approaches.name: Backtracking with In-Place Marking
approaches.explanation: Instead of a separate visited set, temporarily replace each visited cell with a sentinel char (e.g., '#' — anything that can't match a word letter). Restore on backtrack. Saves O(m×n) space. The senior signal — interviewers like seeing the in-place optimization.
